{{Short description|Philosophical and social movement}}
{{Use mdy dates|date=August 2022}}

'''Effective altruism''' ('''EA''') is a 21st-century [[Philosophical movement|philosophical]] and [[social movement]] that advocates impartially calculating benefits and prioritizing causes to provide the greatest good. It is motivated by "using [[Scientific evidence|evidence]] and [[reason]] to figure out how to benefit others as much as possible, and taking action on that basis".<ref name="MacAskill 2017">{{cite journal |last=MacAskill |first=William |date=January 2017 |title=Effective altruism: introduction |url=https://commons.pacificu.edu/eip/vol18/iss1/1 |url-status=live |journal=Essays in Philosophy |language=en |volume=18 |issue=1 |page=eP1580:1–5 |doi=10.7710/1526-0569.1580 |issn=1526-0569 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190807105816/https://commons.pacificu.edu/eip/vol18/iss1/1/ |archive-date=2019-08-07 |access-date=2020-02-08|doi-access=free }}</ref><ref>The quoted definition is endorsed by a number of organizations at: {{cite web |title=CEA's Guiding Principles |url=https://www.centreforeffectivealtruism.org/ceas-guiding-principles |publisher=Centre For Effective Altruism |access-date=2021-12-03}}</ref>
People who pursue the goals of effective altruism, who are sometimes called {{em|effective altruists}},<ref>The term ''effective altruists'' is used to refer to people who embrace effective altruism in many published sources such as {{harvtxt|Oliver|2014}}, {{harvtxt|Singer|2015}}, and {{harvtxt|MacAskill|2017}}, though as {{harvtxt|Pummer|MacAskill|2020}} noted, calling people "effective altruists" minimally means that they are engaged in the project of "using evidence and reason to try to find out how to do the most good, and on this basis trying to do the most good", not that they are perfectly effective nor even that they necessarily participate in the effective altruism community.</ref> follow a variety of approaches proposed by the movement, such as donating to selected [[charities]] and choosing careers, with the goal of maximising positive impact. The movement has spurred the creation of [[research centers]], advisory organizations, and charities, which collectively have donated several hundred million dollars.{{Sfn|Adams|Crary|Gruen|2023|p=xxii}}

A defining feature of effective altruism is impartiality, specifically the global [[equal consideration of interests]] when choosing beneficiaries. Popular [[#Cause priorities|cause priorities]] within effective altruism include [[global health]] and [[Economic development|development]], [[Social inequality|social]] and [[economic inequality]], [[animal welfare]], and [[Global catastrophic risks|risks to the survival or flourishing of humanity]] over the [[Longtermism|long-term future]]. Only a small portion of all charities are affiliated with effective altruism, except in niche areas such as [[Animal welfare#Farmed animals|farmed-animal welfare]], [[AI safety]], and [[biosecurity]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=Piper |first=Kelsey |date=2024-02-09 |title=Can effective altruism stay effective? |url=https://www.vox.com/future-perfect/2024/2/9/24066501/effective-altrusim-philanthropy-artificial-intelligence-ai-safety-pandemics-charity-giving |access-date=2025-05-31 |website=Vox |language=en-US |quote=But within various niche areas that ordinary Americans generally ignore and that have few other funders — like farmed-animal welfare, AI safety, and biosafety and biosecurity — the movement's influence has been much larger.}}</ref>

The movement developed during the 2000s, and the name {{em|effective altruism}} was coined in 2011. Philosophers influential to the movement include [[Peter Singer]], [[Toby Ord]], and [[William MacAskill]]. Effective altruism is most popular within the [[anglosphere]], with concentrations at elite universities in the [[United States]] and [[United Kingdom]], as well as in and around the technology industry in the [[San Francisco Bay Area]].<ref>{{Cite news |last=Tiku |first=Nitasha |date=2023-07-05 |title=How elite schools like Stanford became fixated on the AI apocalypse |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/technology/2023/07/05/ai-apocalypse-college-students/ |access-date=2025-05-31 |newspaper=The Washington Post |language=en-US |issn=0190-8286}}</ref> The movement received mainstream attention and criticism with the [[bankruptcy of FTX|bankruptcy of the cryptocurrency exchange FTX]] as founder [[Sam Bankman-Fried]] was a major funder of effective altruism causes prior to late 2022.<ref name="FTX 2021" /><ref name="Howcroft 2023" />

== History ==
{{Multiple image
| image1            = Peter Singer MIT Veritas.jpg
| image2            = William MacAskill Portrait 2015 (cropped).jpg
| total_width       = 375
| footer            = [[Peter Singer]] and [[William MacAskill]] are among several philosophers who have helped popularize effective altruism.
| alt1              = Picture of Peter Singer
| alt2              = Picture of William MacAskill
}}
Beginning in the latter half of the 2000s, several communities centered around altruist, [[rationalist community|rationalist]], and [[Futures studies|futurological]] concerns started to converge, such as:<ref name="Anthis 2022">{{Cite web |last=Anthis |first=Jayce Reese |date=15 May 2022 |title=Some Early History of Effective Altruism |url=https://jacyanthis.com/some-early-history-of-effective-altruism |access-date=2022-06-03 |website=Jacy Reese Anthis}}</ref>
* The evidence-based charity community centered around [[GiveWell]],<ref>{{Cite news |last=Strom |first=Stephanie |date=2007-12-20 |title=2 Young Hedge-Fund Veterans Stir Up the World of Philanthropy |work=The New York Times |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2007/12/20/us/20charity.html |issn=0362-4331 |access-date=2022-06-03}}</ref> including [[Open Philanthropy]], which originally came out of GiveWell Labs but then became independent.<ref name="Matthews 2015b">{{cite web |last=Matthews |first=Dylan |author-link=Dylan Matthews |date=April 24, 2015 |title=You have $8 billion. You want to do as much good as possible. What do you do? Inside the Open Philanthropy Project |url=https://www.vox.com/2015/4/24/8457895/givewell-open-philanthropy-charity |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170824133751/https://www.vox.com/2015/4/24/8457895/givewell-open-philanthropy-charity |archive-date=August 24, 2017 |access-date=April 27, 2015 |website=[[Vox (website)|Vox]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |last=Cha |first=Ariana Eunjung |date=2014-12-26 |title=Cari Tuna and Dustin Moskovitz: Young Silicon Valley billionaires pioneer new approach to philanthropy |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/business/billionaire-couple-give-plenty-to-charity-but-they-do-quite-a-bit-of-homework/2014/12/26/19fae34c-86d6-11e4-b9b7-b8632ae73d25_story.html |access-date=2022-02-06}}</ref>
* The community around pledging and career selection for effective giving, centered around the [[Giving What We Can]] and [[80,000 Hours]] nonprofits.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Christian |first=Alex |date=10 October 2023 |title=FTX's Sam Bankman-Fried believed in 'effective altruism'. What is it? |url=https://www.bbc.com/worklife/article/20231009-ftxs-sam-bankman-fried-believed-in-effective-altruism-what-is-it |access-date=2024-05-16 |website=BBC |language=en-GB}}</ref>
* The [[Singularity Institute]] (now [[Machine Intelligence Research Institute|MIRI]]) for studying the [[AI alignment|safety of artificial intelligence]], the [[Future of Humanity Institute]] studying topics such as [[existential risk]], and the ''[[LessWrong]]'' discussion forum, which focuses on rationalism.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Chivers |first=Tom |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=mMzdtAEACAAJ |title=The AI Does Not Hate You: The Rationalists and Their Quest to Save the World |publisher=Weidenfeld & Nicolson |year=2019 |isbn=978-1-4746-0877-0 |language=en |chapter=The Effective Altruists}}</ref>

In 2011, Giving What We Can and 80,000 Hours decided to incorporate into an [[umbrella organization]] and held a vote for their new name; the "Centre for Effective Altruism" was selected.<ref>{{multiref2 | {{cite news |last=MacAskill |first=William |author-link=William MacAskill |date=March 10, 2014 |title=The history of the term 'effective altruism' |url=https://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/9a7xMXoSiQs3EYPA2/the-history-of-the-term-effective-altruism |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200220215628/https://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/9a7xMXoSiQs3EYPA2/the-history-of-the-term-effective-altruism |archive-date=February 20, 2020 |access-date=February 2, 2020 |website=Effective Altruism Forum}} | {{cite news |last1=Ram |first1=Aliya |date=December 4, 2015 |title=The power and efficacy of effective altruism |work=Financial Times |url=https://www.ft.com/content/1954323c-8eb3-11e5-8be4-3506bf20cc2b |url-status=live |access-date=February 14, 2018 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180806145437/https://www.ft.com/content/1954323c-8eb3-11e5-8be4-3506bf20cc2b |archive-date=August 6, 2018}} }}</ref><ref name="Singer 2015">{{cite book |last=Singer |first=Peter |title=[[The Most Good You Can Do|The Most Good You Can Do: How Effective Altruism Is Changing Ideas About Living Ethically]] |date=2015 |publisher=[[Yale University Press]] |isbn=9780300180275 |series=Castle lectures in ethics, politics, and economics |location=New Haven |oclc=890614537}}</ref> The [[Effective Altruism Global]] conference has been held since 2013. As the movement formed, it attracted individuals who were not part of a specific community, but who had been following the Australian moral philosopher [[Peter Singer]]'s work on [[applied ethics]], particularly "[[Famine, Affluence, and Morality]]" (1972), ''[[Animal Liberation (book)|Animal Liberation]]'' (1975), and ''[[The Life You Can Save]]'' (2009).<ref name="Anthis 2022" /><ref name="Lichtenberg 2015"/> Singer himself used the term in 2013, in a [[TED talk]] titled "The Why and How of Effective Altruism".<ref>{{Cite news |last=Gose |first=Ben |date=April 5, 2015 |title=Don't Let Passion Guide Giving, Peter Singer Says |url=https://www.philanthropy.com/article/dont-let-passion-guide-giving-peter-singer-says/ |work=The Chronicle of Philanthropy}}</ref>

=== Notable philanthropists ===
An estimated $416 million was donated to effective charities identified by the movement in 2019,<ref>{{cite web |last=Todd |first=Benjamin |date=2020-08-09 |title=How are resources in effective altruism allocated across issues? |url=https://80000hours.org/2021/08/effective-altruism-allocation-resources-cause-areas/ |access-date=2021-12-15 |website=[[80,000 Hours]]}}</ref> representing a 37% annual growth rate since 2015.<ref>{{cite web |last=Todd |first=Benjamin |date=2021-07-28 |title=Is effective altruism growing? An update on the stock of funding vs. people |url=https://80000hours.org/2021/07/effective-altruism-growing/ |access-date=2021-12-15 |website=[[80,000 Hours]]}}</ref> Two of the largest donors in the effective altruism community, [[Dustin Moskovitz]], who had become wealthy through co-founding Facebook, and his wife [[Cari Tuna]], hope to donate most of their net worth of over $11 billion for effective altruist causes through the private foundation [[Good Ventures]] and are major funders of [[Open Philanthropy]].<ref name="Matthews 2015b" /> Others influenced by effective altruism include Sam Bankman-Fried,<ref>{{cite magazine |last=Zillman |first=Claire |date=29 July 2021 |title=Sam Bankman-Fried and the conscience of a crypto billionaire |url=https://caritafoundation.world/top-charities-for-climate-change-donation-2023/ |access-date=2021-12-06 |magazine=[[Fortune (magazine)|Fortune]]}}</ref> and professional poker players [[Dan Smith (poker player)|Dan Smith]]<ref name="Pincus-Roth 2020">{{cite magazine |last=Pincus-Roth |first=Zachary |author-link=Zachary Pincus-Roth |date=23 September 2020 |title=The Rise of the Rational Do-Gooders |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/magazine/2020/09/23/effective-altruism-charity/ |access-date=2021-12-06 |magazine=[[The Washington Post Magazine]]}}</ref> and [[Liv Boeree]].<ref name="Pincus-Roth 2020" /> [[Jaan Tallinn]], the Estonian billionaire founder of Skype, is known for donating to some effective altruist causes.<ref name="Matthews 2015a" /> Sam Bankman-Fried launched a philanthropic organization called the FTX Foundation in February 2021,<ref name="FTX 2021">{{cite web |author=FTX |title=The FTX Foundation for Charitable Giving |url=https://ftx.medium.com/the-ftx-foundation-for-charitable-giving-5ae53178dce |website=ftx.medium.com |date=8 February 2021 |access-date=2024-02-29 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210208055942/https://ftx.medium.com/the-ftx-foundation-for-charitable-giving-5ae53178dce |archive-date=2021-02-08 |url-status=live}}</ref> and it made contributions to a number of effective altruist organizations, but it was shut down in November 2022 when FTX collapsed.<ref name="Howcroft 2023">{{cite news |last=Howcroft |first=Elizabeth |date=6 April 2023 |title=Collapse of FTX deprives academics of grants, stokes fears of forced repayment |url=https://www.reuters.com/technology/collapse-ftx-deprives-academics-grants-stokes-fears-forced-repayment-2023-04-06/ |work=[[Reuters]] |access-date=2024-02-29}}</ref>

=== Notable publications and media ===
A number of books and articles related to effective altruism have been published that have codified, criticized, and brought more attention to the movement. In 2015, philosopher [[Peter Singer]] published ''[[The Most Good You Can Do: How Effective Altruism Is Changing Ideas About Living Ethically]]''.<ref name="Kristof 2015">{{cite news |last=Kristof |first=Nicholas |author-link=Nicholas Kristof |date=April 4, 2015 |title=The Trader Who Donates Half His Pay |newspaper=[[The New York Times]] |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2015/04/05/opinion/sunday/nicholas-kristof-the-trader-who-donates-half-his-pay.html |url-status=live |access-date=April 11, 2015 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191009152730/https://www.nytimes.com/2015/04/05/opinion/sunday/nicholas-kristof-the-trader-who-donates-half-his-pay.html |archive-date=October 9, 2019}}</ref> The same year, the Scottish philosopher and ethicist [[William MacAskill]] published ''[[Doing Good Better|Doing Good Better: How Effective Altruism Can Help You Make a Difference]]''.<ref>{{multiref2 | {{Cite news |last=Shariatmadari |first=David |date=August 20, 2015 |title=Doing Good Better by William MacAskill review – if you read this book, you'll change the charities you donate to |language=en-GB |work=The Guardian |url=https://www.theguardian.com/books/2015/aug/20/doing-good-better-william-macaskill-review |url-status=live |access-date=May 21, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170622131310/https://www.theguardian.com/books/2015/aug/20/doing-good-better-william-macaskill-review |archive-date=June 22, 2017 |issn=0261-3077}} | {{Cite news |last=Cowen |first=Tyler |author-link=Tyler Cowen |date=August 14, 2015 |title=Effective Altruism: Where Charity and Rationality Meet |work=[[The New York Times]] |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2015/08/16/upshot/effective-altruism-where-charity-and-rationality-meet.html |url-status=live |access-date=May 21, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161221213814/https://www.nytimes.com/2015/08/16/upshot/effective-altruism-where-charity-and-rationality-meet.html |archive-date=December 21, 2016 |issn=0362-4331}} }}</ref><ref name="Thompson 2015">{{cite news |last1=Thompson|first1=Derek |author-link=Derek Thompson (journalist)|date=June 15, 2015 |title=The Greatest Good |work=[[The Atlantic]] |url=https://www.theatlantic.com/business/archive/2015/06/what-is-the-greatest-good/395768/ |url-status=live |access-date=March 6, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190820061858/https://www.theatlantic.com/business/archive/2015/06/what-is-the-greatest-good/395768/ |archive-date=August 20, 2019}}</ref> B.V.E. Hyde describes these two books as "the bibles of effective altruism".<ref name="Hyde 2025" />
In 2018, American news website ''[[Vox (website)|Vox]]'' launched its ''Future Perfect'' section, led by journalist [[Dylan Matthews]], which publishes articles and podcasts on "finding the best ways to do good".<ref>{{cite web |last=Schmidt |first=Christine |date=October 15, 2018 |title=Will Vox's new section on effective altruism... well, do any good? |url=https://www.niemanlab.org/2018/10/will-voxs-new-section-on-effective-altruism-well-do-any-good/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200201145307/https://www.niemanlab.org/2018/10/will-voxs-new-section-on-effective-altruism-well-do-any-good/ |archive-date=February 1, 2020 |access-date=February 1, 2020 |website=[[Nieman Journalism Lab]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=Matthews |first=Dylan|author-link=Dylan Matthews |date=October 15, 2018 |title=Future Perfect, explained |url=https://www.vox.com/future-perfect/2018/10/15/17924288/future-perfect-explained |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191225210922/https://www.vox.com/future-perfect/2018/10/15/17924288/future-perfect-explained |archive-date=December 25, 2019 |access-date=December 8, 2018 |website=[[Vox (website)|Vox]]}} Some topics that the ''Future Perfect'' series has covered include:
* Effective philanthropy: {{Cite web |last=Matthews|first=Dylan|author-link=Dylan Matthews|date=December 17, 2019 |title=These are the charities where your money will do the most good |url=https://www.vox.com/2015/12/1/9826838/best-charities-donate-2018-giving-tuesday |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190829075642/https://www.vox.com/2015/12/1/9826838/best-charities-donate-2018-giving-tuesday |archive-date=August 29, 2019 |access-date=February 1, 2020 |website=Vox}}
* High-impact career choice: {{Cite web |last=Matthews |first=Dylan |author-link=Dylan Matthews |date=November 28, 2018a |title=How to pick a career that counts |url=https://www.vox.com/future-perfect/2018/11/28/18114601/future-perfect-podcast-career-choice |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191224101233/https://www.vox.com/future-perfect/2018/11/28/18114601/future-perfect-podcast-career-choice |archive-date=December 24, 2019 |access-date=December 8, 2018 |website=Vox}}
* [[Poverty reduction]] through [[women's empowerment]]: {{Cite web |last=Illing |first=Sean |date=March 8, 2019 |title=Want less poverty in the world? Empower women. |url=https://www.vox.com/2018/11/5/18037688/gender-equality-poverty-human-rights |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181209124140/https://www.vox.com/2018/11/5/18037688/gender-equality-poverty-human-rights |archive-date=December 9, 2018 |access-date=February 1, 2020 |website=Vox}}
* Improving children's learning efficiently through improving [[environmental health]]: {{Cite web |last=Yglesias|first=Matthew |author-link=Matthew Yglesias |date=January 8, 2020 |title=Installing air filters in classrooms has surprisingly large educational benefits: $1,000 can raise a class's test scores by as much as cutting class size by a third |url=https://www.vox.com/2020/1/8/21051869/indoor-air-pollution-student-achievement |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200201145308/https://www.vox.com/2020/1/8/21051869/indoor-air-pollution-student-achievement |archive-date=February 1, 2020 |access-date=February 1, 2020 |website=Vox}}
* [[Animal welfare]] improvements: {{Cite web |last=Piper |first=Kelsey |date=November 27, 2018a |title=Where will your donations do the most for animals? |url=https://www.vox.com/2018/11/27/18111908/giving-tuesday-best-animal-charity |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181128115806/https://www.vox.com/2018/11/27/18111908/giving-tuesday-best-animal-charity |archive-date=November 28, 2018 |access-date=December 8, 2018 |website=Vox}}
* Ways to reduce [[global catastrophic risk]]s: {{Cite news |last=Piper |first=Kelsey |date=November 19, 2018 |title=How technological progress is making it likelier than ever that humans will destroy ourselves |work=Vox |url=https://www.vox.com/future-perfect/2018/11/19/18097663/nick-bostrom-vulnerable-world-global-catastrophic-risks |url-status=live |access-date=December 9, 2018 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181209070423/https://www.vox.com/future-perfect/2018/11/19/18097663/nick-bostrom-vulnerable-world-global-catastrophic-risks |archive-date=December 9, 2018}}</ref>
In 2019, [[Oxford University Press]] published the volume ''Effective Altruism: Philosophical Issues'', edited by [[Hilary Greaves]] and Theron Pummer.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://global.oup.com/academic/product/effective-altruism-9780198841364?cc=us&lang=en& |title=Effective Altruism: Philosophical Issues |date=2019-11-15 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-884136-4 |editor-last=Greaves |editor-first=Hilary |series=Engaging Philosophy |location=Oxford, England; New York |language=en |editor-last2=Pummer |editor-first2=Theron}}</ref> More recent books have emphasized concerns for future generations. In 2020, the Australian moral philosopher [[Toby Ord]] published ''[[The Precipice: Existential Risk and the Future of Humanity]]'',<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Pummer |first=Theron |date=2020-08-02 |title=The Precipice: Existential Risk and the Future of Humanity |url=https://ndpr.nd.edu/reviews/the-precipice-existential-risk-and-the-future-of-humanity/ |access-date=2022-08-13 |website=Notre Dame Philosophical Reviews |language=en}}</ref> while MacAskill published ''[[What We Owe the Future]]'' in 2022.<ref name="MacAskill 2022b">{{Cite book |last=MacAskill |first=William |author-link=William MacAskill |title=What We Owe the Future |date=2022 |publisher=Basic Books |isbn=978-1-5416-1862-6 |oclc=1314633519}}</ref> In 2023, Oxford University Press published the volume ''The Good it Promises, The Harm it Does: Critical Essays on Effective Altruism'', edited by [[Carol J. Adams]], [[Alice Crary]], and [[Lori Gruen]].<ref>{{cite book |editor1-last=Adams |editor1-first=Carol J. |editor1-link=Carol J. Adams |editor2-last=Crary |editor2-first=Alice |editor2-link=Alice Crary |editor3-last=Gruen |editor3-first=Lori |editor3-link=Lori Gruen  |date=2023 |title=The Good it Promises, The Harm it Does: Critical Essays on Effective Altruism |location=Oxford; New York |publisher=[[Oxford University Press]] |isbn=9780197655702 |oclc=1350838764 |doi=10.1093/oso/9780197655696.001.0001 |ref={{harvid|Adams|Crary|Gruen|2023}}}}</ref><ref name="Pettigrew 2023">{{cite journal |last=Pettigrew |first=Richard |date=27 September 2023 |title=Review of Carol J. Adams, Alice Crary, and Lori Gruen (eds.) ''The Good It Promises, The Harm It Does: Critical Essays on Effective Altruism'', 2023, Oxford: Oxford University Press. |journal=[[Mind (journal)|Mind]] |doi=10.1093/mind/fzad047}}</ref>

== Philosophy ==

Effective altruists focus on the many philosophical questions related to the most effective ways to benefit others.<ref name="Pummer 2020">{{cite encyclopedia |title=Effective altruism |encyclopedia=International Encyclopedia of Ethics |publisher=[[John Wiley & Sons]] |location=Hoboken, New Jersey |url=https://philarchive.org/archive/PUMEA |date=June 2020 |editor-last=LaFollette |editor-first=Hugh |pages=1–9 |doi=10.1002/9781444367072.wbiee883 |isbn=9781444367072 |oclc=829259960 |last2=MacAskill |first2=William |last1=Pummer |first1=Theron |s2cid=241220220}}</ref><ref name="MacAskill 2019">{{cite book |last=MacAskill |first=William |title=Effective Altruism: Philosophical Issues |date=2019a |publisher=[[Oxford University Press]] |isbn=9780198841364 |editor1-last=Greaves |editor1-first=Hilary |editor1-link=Hilary Greaves |series=Engaging philosophy |location=Oxford; New York |pages=10–28 |chapter=The definition of effective altruism |doi=10.1093/oso/9780198841364.003.0001 |oclc=1101772304 |author-link= |editor2-last=Pummer |editor2-first=Theron}}</ref> Such philosophical questions shift the starting point of reasoning from "what to do" to "why" and "how".<ref name="Crouch 2013">{{cite web |last=Crouch |first=Will |date=May 30, 2013 |title=What is effective altruism? |url=http://blog.practicalethics.ox.ac.uk/2013/05/what-is-effective-altruism/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151003212110/http://blog.practicalethics.ox.ac.uk/2013/05/what-is-effective-altruism/ |archive-date=October 3, 2015 |access-date=February 4, 2020 |website=Practical Ethics Blog |publisher=[[University of Oxford]]}}</ref> There is not a consensus on the answers, and there are also differences between effective altruists who believe that they should do the most good they possibly can with all of their resources<ref>{{harvtxt|Singer|2015}} expressed a clearly normative view: "Effective altruism is based on a very simple idea: we should do the most good we can. Obeying the usual rules about not stealing, cheating, hurting, and killing is not enough, or at least not enough for those of us who have the great good fortune to live in material comfort, who can feed, house, and clothe ourselves and our families and still have money or time to spare. Living a minimally acceptable ethical life involves using a substantial part of our spare resources to make the world a better place. Living a fully ethical life involves doing the most good we can." (p.&nbsp;vii)</ref> and those who only try do the most good they can within a defined budget.<ref name="MacAskill 2019" />{{rp|15}}

According to MacAskill, the view of effective altruism as doing the most good one can within a defined budget can be compatible with a wide variety of views on [[morality]] and [[meta-ethics]], as well as traditional religious teachings on altruism such as in [[Christianity]].<ref name="MacAskill 2017" /><ref name="Pummer 2020" /> Effective altruism can also be in tension with religion where religion emphasizes spending resources on worship and evangelism instead of causes that do the most good.<ref name="MacAskill 2017" /> Other than Peter Singer and MacAskill, philosophers associated with effective altruism include [[Nick Bostrom]],<ref name="Matthews 2015a">{{Cite web |last=Matthews |first=Dylan |date=2015-08-10 |title=I spent a weekend at Google talking with nerds about charity. I came away ... worried. |url=https://www.vox.com/2015/8/10/9124145/effective-altruism-global-ai |access-date=2022-08-13 |website=Vox |language=en}}</ref> [[Toby Ord]],<ref>{{cite magazine |last=Bajekal |first=Naina |date=22–29 August 2022 |title=How to do the most good: a growing movement argues we should care about people thousands of miles away—and millions of years in the future |magazine=[[Time (magazine)|Time]] |volume=200 |issue=7–8 |pages=69–75 |url=https://time.com/6204627/effective-altruism-longtermism-william-macaskill-interview/}}</ref> [[Hilary Greaves]],<ref>{{Cite web |title=Hilary Greaves |url=https://www.philosophy.ox.ac.uk/people/hilary-greaves |access-date=2022-08-13 |website=Faculty of Philosophy |publisher=University of Oxford |language=en}}</ref> and [[Derek Parfit]].<ref>{{Cite news |last=O'Grady |first=Jane |date=2017-01-12 |title=Derek Parfit obituary |language=en-GB |work=The Guardian |url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/2017/jan/12/derek-parfit-obituary |access-date=2017-07-03 |issn=0261-3077}}</ref> Economist [[Yew-Kwang Ng]] conducted similar research in [[welfare economics]] and [[moral philosophy]].<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Wiblin |first1=Robert |last2=Harris |first2=Keiran |date=2018-07-26 |title=Prof Yew-Kwang Ng on ethics and how to create a much happier world |url=https://80000hours.org/podcast/episodes/yew-kwang-ng-anticipating-effective-altruism/ |access-date=2022-08-13 |website=80,000 Hours |language=en-US}}</ref>

The Centre for Effective Altruism lists the following four principles that unite effective altruism: prioritization, impartial altruism, open truthseeking, and a collaborative spirit.<ref name="Centre for Effective Altruism">{{Cite web |title=What is effective altruism? |url=https://www.effectivealtruism.org/articles/introduction-to-effective-altruism#what-principles-unite-effective-altruism |access-date=2023-11-30 |language=en |publisher=Centre for Effective Altruism |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231129125601/https://www.effectivealtruism.org/articles/introduction-to-effective-altruism#what-principles-unite-effective-altruism |archive-date=2023-11-29 |url-status=live}} These four principles were first called "values" and were added to the cited web page sometime between [https://web.archive.org/web/20220727220519/https://www.effectivealtruism.org/articles/introduction-to-effective-altruism {{date|2022-07-27|MDY}}] and [https://web.archive.org/web/20220802230238/https://www.effectivealtruism.org/articles/introduction-to-effective-altruism {{date|2022-08-02|MDY}}].</ref> To support people's ability to act altruistically on the basis of impartial reasoning, the effective altruism movement promotes values and actions such as a collaborative spirit, honesty, transparency, and publicly pledging to donate a certain percentage of income or other resources.<ref name="MacAskill 2017"/>{{rp|2}}

===Impartiality===
{{See also|Equal consideration of interests}}

Effective altruism aims to emphasize impartial reasoning in that everyone's [[well-being]] counts equally.<ref>{{harvnb|Singer|2015|pp=85–95}}; {{harvnb|MacAskill|2019a|pp=17–19}}; {{harvnb|Pummer|MacAskill|2020}}.</ref> Singer, in his 1972 essay "[[Famine, Affluence, and Morality]]",<ref name="Lichtenberg 2015">On the influence of Singer's essay "[[Famine, Affluence, and Morality]]" see, for example: {{harvnb|Snow|2015}}, {{harvnb|Singer|2015|pp=13–20}}, and {{cite magazine |last=Lichtenberg |first=Judith |date=November 30, 2015 |title=Peter Singer's extremely altruistic heirs: Forty years after it was written, 'Famine, Affluence, and Morality' has spawned a radical new movement |url=https://newrepublic.com/article/124690/peter-singers-extremely-altruistic-heirs |magazine=[[The New Republic]]}} Singer's arguments for impartiality were later repeated in other books by him (such as {{harvnb|Singer|2009}}, {{harvnb|Singer|2015}}).</ref> wrote:

<blockquote>It makes no moral difference whether the person I can help is a neighbor's child ten yards away from me or a Bengali whose name I shall never know, ten thousand miles away&nbsp;... The moral point of view requires us to look beyond the interests of our own society.<ref name="Singer 1972">{{cite journal |last=Singer |first=Peter |date=Spring 1972 |title=Famine, Affluence, and Morality |journal=[[Philosophy and Public Affairs]] |volume=1 |issue=3 |pages=229–243 |jstor=2265052}} The essay was republished in book form in 2016 with a new preface and two extra essays by Singer: {{cite book |last=Singer |first=Peter |title=Famine, Affluence, and Morality |date=2016 |publisher=[[Oxford University Press]] |isbn=9780190219208 |location=Oxford; New York |oclc=907446001}}</ref>{{rp|231–232}}</blockquote>

Impartiality combined with seeking to do the most good leads to prioritizing benefits to those who are in a worse state, because anyone who happens to be worse off will benefit more from an improvement in their state, all other things being equal.<ref name="Pummer 2020"/><ref name="Centre for Effective Altruism"/>

====Scope of moral consideration====
One issue related to moral impartiality is the question of which beings are deserving of moral consideration. Some effective altruists consider the well-being of non-human animals in addition to humans, and advocate for animal welfare issues such as ending [[factory farming]].<ref>{{multiref2 | {{cite journal |last=Fisher |first=Andrew |date=January 2017 |title=Theory-neutral arguments for 'effective animal advocacy' |url=https://commons.pacificu.edu/eip/vol18/iss1/3 |url-status=live |journal=Essays in Philosophy |volume=18 |issue=1 |pages=eP1578:1–14 |doi=10.7710/1526-0569.1578 |issn=1526-0569 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190807105844/https://commons.pacificu.edu/eip/vol18/iss1/3/ |archive-date=2019-08-07 |access-date=2020-02-08|doi-access=free |url-access=subscription }} | {{cite journal |last=Broad |first=Garrett M. |date=December 2018 |title=Effective animal advocacy: effective altruism, the social economy, and the animal protection movement |journal=Agriculture and Human Values |volume=35 |issue=4 |pages=777–789 |doi=10.1007/s10460-018-9873-5 |s2cid=158634567}} }}</ref> Those who subscribe to [[longtermism]] include [[future generations]] as possible beneficiaries and try to improve the moral value of the long-term future, for example by reducing [[existential risks]].<ref name="Singer 2015" />{{rp|165–178}}<ref>{{cite book |last=Beckstead |first=Nick |title=Effective Altruism: Philosophical Issues |date=2019 |publisher=[[Oxford University Press]] |isbn=9780198841364 |editor1-last=Greaves |editor1-first=Hilary |editor1-link=Hilary Greaves |series=Engaging philosophy |location=Oxford, England; New York |pages=80–98 |chapter=A brief argument for the overwhelming importance of shaping the far future |doi=10.1093/oso/9780198841364.003.0006 |oclc=1101772304 |editor2-last=Pummer |editor2-first=Theron}}</ref>

====Criticism of impartiality====
The [[drowning child analogy]] in Singer's essay provoked philosophical debate. In response to a version of Singer's drowning child analogy,<ref>{{cite web |last1=Zwolinski |first1=Matt |title=Why Wouldn't You Save a Drowning Child? |url=https://fee.org/articles/why-wouldnt-you-save-a-drowning-child/ |website=[[Foundation for Economic Education]] |language=en |date=24 August 2015}}</ref> philosopher [[Kwame Anthony Appiah]] in 2006 asked whether the most effective action of a man in an expensive suit, confronted with a drowning child, would not be to save the child and ruin his suit—but rather, sell the suit and donate the proceeds to charity.<ref name="Appiah 2006">{{cite book |last=Appiah |first=Kwame Anthony |author-link=Kwame Anthony Appiah |date=2006 |title=Cosmopolitanism: Ethics in a World of Strangers |series=Issues of Our Time |location=New York |publisher=[[W. W. Norton & Company]] |pages=[https://archive.org/details/cosmopolitanisme0000appi_v9i3/page/158 158–162] |isbn=0393061558 |oclc=61445790 |url=https://archive.org/details/cosmopolitanisme0000appi_v9i3 |url-access=registration}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last1=Mclauchlan |first1=Danyl |title=In search of a way to do good that amounts to more than feeling good |url=https://thespinoff.co.nz/politics/08-04-2019/in-search-of-a-way-to-do-good-that-amounts-to-more-than-feeling-good |website=[[The Spinoff]] |access-date=19 December 2022 |language=en |date=8 April 2019}}</ref> Appiah believed that he "should save the drowning child and ruin my suit".<ref name="Appiah 2006"/> In a 2015 debate, when presented with a similar scenario of either saving a child from a burning building or saving a [[Picasso]] painting to sell and donate the proceeds to charity, MacAskill responded that the effective altruist should save and sell the Picasso.<ref>{{cite AV media |title=Effective Altruism: A Better Way to Lead an Ethical Life |date=November 30, 2015 |publisher=Intelligence Squared |url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Qslo4-DpzPs&t=1265s |time=21:05 |via=[[YouTube]] |access-date=January 23, 2022}}</ref> Psychologist Alan Jern called MacAskill's choice "unnatural, even distasteful, to many people", although Jern concluded that effective altruism raises questions "worth asking".<ref>{{cite web |last=Jern |first=Alan |date=October 13, 2020 |title=Effective altruism is logical, but too unnatural to catch on |url=https://psyche.co/ideas/effective-altruism-is-logical-but-too-unnatural-to-catch-on |website=[[Aeon (magazine)|Psyche.co]] |access-date=January 23, 2022}}</ref> MacAskill later endorsed a "qualified definition of effective altruism" in which effective altruists try to do the most good "without violating constraints" such as any obligations that someone might have to help those nearby.<ref name="MacAskill 2019a">{{harvnb|MacAskill|2019a|pp=15–20}}, especially section 4.1, "Misconception #1: Effective altruism is just utilitarianism"; {{harvnb|Pummer|MacAskill|2020}}.</ref>

William Schambra has criticized the impartial logic of effective altruism, arguing that benevolence arising from [[Reciprocity (social psychology)|reciprocity]] and face-to-face interactions is stronger and more prevalent than charity based on impartial, detached altruism.<ref name="Schambra 2014"/> Such community-based charitable giving, he wrote, is foundational to [[civil society]] and, in turn, [[democracy]].<ref name="Schambra 2014">{{cite web |last=Schambra |first=William A. |date=May 22, 2014 |title=Opinion: The coming showdown between philanthrolocalism and effective altruism |url=https://www.philanthropydaily.com/the-coming-showdown-between-philanthrolocalism-and-effective-altruism/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200205205424/https://www.philanthropydaily.com/the-coming-showdown-between-philanthrolocalism-and-effective-altruism/ |archive-date=February 5, 2020 |access-date=February 5, 2020 |website=Philanthropy Daily }}</ref> [[Larissa MacFarquhar]] said that people have diverse moral emotions, and she suggested that some effective altruists are not unemotional and detached but feel as much empathy for distant strangers as for people nearby.<ref>{{cite web |last=MacFarquhar |first=Larissa |author-link=Larissa MacFarquhar |date=July 1, 2015 |url=https://www.bostonreview.net/forum_response/response-larissa-macfarquhar/ |title=Forum Response: Response to Effective Altruism: Altruism at a distance need not require choosing logic over empathy |website=[[Boston Review]]}}</ref> Richard Pettigrew concurred that many effective altruists "feel more profound dismay at the suffering of people unknown to them than many people feel", and he argued that impartiality in EA need not be dispassionate and "is not obviously in tension with much in [[care ethics]]" as some philosophers have argued.<ref name="Pettigrew 2023" /> [[Ross Douthat]] of ''The New York Times'' criticized the movement's {{" '}}telescopic philanthropy' aimed at distant populations" and envisioned "effective altruists sitting around in a San Francisco skyscraper calculating how to relieve suffering halfway around the world while the city decays beneath them", while he also praised the movement for providing "useful rebukes to the solipsism and anti-human pessimism that haunts the developed world today".<ref>{{cite news |last1=Douthat |first1=Ross |title=Opinion {{!}} The Case for a Less-Effective Altruism |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2022/11/18/opinion/effective-altruism.html |access-date=19 December 2022 |work=The New York Times |date=18 November 2022}}</ref>

===Cause prioritization===
A key component of effective altruism is "cause prioritization". Cause prioritization is based on the principle of cause ''neutrality'', the idea that resources should be distributed to causes based on what will do the most good, irrespective of the identity of the beneficiary and the way in which they are helped.<ref name="Pummer 2020" /> By contrast, many non-profits emphasize effectiveness and evidence with respect to a single cause such as education or climate change.<ref name="Schambra 2014"/>

One tool that EA-based organizations may use to prioritize cause areas is the {{em|importance, tractability, and neglectedness}} framework. Importance is the amount of value that would be created if a problem were solved, tractability is the fraction of a problem that would be solved if additional resources were devoted to it, and neglectedness is the quantity of resources already committed to a cause.<ref name="Lewis-Kraus 2022b">{{Cite magazine| issn = 0028-792X| last = Lewis-Kraus| first = Gideon| title = The Reluctant Prophet of Effective Altruism| magazine = The New Yorker| access-date = 2022-12-04| date = 2022-08-08| url = https://www.newyorker.com/magazine/2022/08/15/the-reluctant-prophet-of-effective-altruism}}</ref>

The information required for cause prioritization may involve [[data analysis]], comparing possible outcomes with what would have happened under other conditions ([[#Counterfactual reasoning|counterfactual reasoning]]), and identifying [[uncertainty]].<ref name="Pummer 2020" /><ref>{{cite journal |last=MacAskill |first=William |author-link=William MacAskill |date=September 2019b |title=Practical ethics given moral uncertainty |url=https://ora.ox.ac.uk/objects/uuid:f3da6f54-8017-432a-a294-7d7707e6a74d |url-status=live |journal=[[Utilitas]] |volume=31 |issue=3 |pages=231–245 |doi=10.1017/S0953820819000013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210215082736/https://ora.ox.ac.uk/objects/uuid:f3da6f54-8017-432a-a294-7d7707e6a74d |archive-date=2021-02-15 |access-date=2020-09-19 |s2cid=150859616}}</ref> The difficulty of these tasks has led to the creation of organizations that specialize in researching the relative prioritization of causes.<ref name="Pummer 2020" />

====Criticism of cause prioritization====
This practice of "weighing causes and beneficiaries against one another" was criticized by Ken Berger and Robert Penna of [[Charity Navigator]] for being "moralistic, in the worst sense of the word" and "elitist".<ref>{{cite magazine|url=https://ssir.org/articles/entry/the_elitist_philanthropy_of_so_called_effective_altruism|title=The Elitist Philanthropy of So-Called Effective Altruism|last1=Berger|first1=Ken|last2=Penna|first2=Robert|date=November 25, 2013|magazine=[[Stanford Social Innovation Review]]|access-date=2021-12-05|archive-date=2021-12-03|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211203014448/https://ssir.org/articles/entry/the_elitist_philanthropy_of_so_called_effective_altruism|url-status=live}}</ref> William MacAskill responded to Berger and Penna, defending the rationale for comparing one beneficiary's interests against another and concluding that such comparison is difficult and sometimes impossible but often necessary.<ref name="MacAskill 2013">{{cite magazine|url=https://ssir.org/articles/entry/what_charity_navigator_gets_wrong_about_effective_altruism|title=What Charity Navigator Gets Wrong About Effective Altruism|last=MacAskill|first=William|date=December 3, 2013|magazine=[[Stanford Social Innovation Review]]|access-date=2021-12-05|archive-date=2021-12-03|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211203054941/https://ssir.org/articles/entry/what_charity_navigator_gets_wrong_about_effective_altruism|url-status=live}}</ref> MacAskill argued that the more pernicious form of elitism was that of donating to art galleries (and like institutions) instead of charity.<ref name="MacAskill 2013"/> Ian David Moss suggested that the criticism of cause prioritization could be resolved by what he called "domain-specific effective altruism", which would encourage "that principles of effective altruism be followed within an area of philanthropic focus, such as a specific cause or geography" and could resolve the conflict between local and global perspectives for some donors.<ref>{{cite magazine |last1=Moss |first1=Ian David |date=Spring 2017 |title=In Defense of Pet Causes |url=https://ssir.org/articles/entry/in_defense_of_pet_causes |magazine=[[Stanford Social Innovation Review]] |access-date=19 December 2022 |language=en-us}}</ref>

===Cost-effectiveness===
Some charities are considered to be far more effective than others, as charities may spend different amounts of money to achieve the same goal, and some charities may not achieve the goal at all.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Thompson |first=Derek |date=2015-06-15 |title=The Most Efficient Way to Save a Life |url=https://www.theatlantic.com/business/archive/2015/06/what-is-the-greatest-good/395768/ |access-date=2023-04-20 |website=The Atlantic |language=en}}</ref> Effective altruists seek to identify interventions that are highly cost-effective in [[expected value|expectation]]. Many interventions have [[Uncertainty|uncertain]] benefits, and the expected value of one intervention can be higher than that of another if its benefits are larger, even if it has a smaller chance of succeeding.<ref name="Thompson 2015" /> One metric effective altruists use to choose between health interventions is the estimated number of [[quality-adjusted life year]]s (QALY) added per dollar.<ref name="Lewis-Kraus 2022b"/>

Some effective altruist organizations prefer [[randomized controlled trial]]s as a primary form of evidence,<ref name="Thompson 2015" /><ref name="Skelton 2016">{{cite journal |last1=Skelton |first1=Anthony |date=2016 |title=The ethical principles of effective altruism |url=https://philpapers.org/rec/SKETEP |url-status=live |journal=[[Journal of Global Ethics]] |volume=12 |issue=2 |pages=137–146 |doi=10.1080/17449626.2016.1193552 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170312072508/https://philpapers.org/rec/SKETEP |archive-date=March 12, 2017 |access-date=March 11, 2017 |s2cid=147936480|url-access=subscription }}</ref> as they are commonly considered the highest level of evidence in healthcare research.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://nhmrc.gov.au/about-us/publications/guide-development-evaluation-and-implementation-clinical-practice-guidelines |title=A guide to the development, implementation and evaluation of clinical practice guidelines |publisher=National Health and Medical Research Council |year=1998 |isbn=1864960485 |location=Canberra, Commonwealth of Australia |pages=21–25 |language=en |access-date=2019-01-12 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190113075529/https://nhmrc.gov.au/about-us/publications/guide-development-evaluation-and-implementation-clinical-practice-guidelines |archive-date=2019-01-13 |url-status=live}}</ref> Others have argued that requiring this stringent level of evidence unnecessarily narrows the focus to issues where the evidence can be developed.<ref name="Rubenstein 2016">{{cite web |last1=Rubenstein |first1=Jennifer |date=14 December 2016 |title=The Lessons of Effective Altruism |url=https://www.ethicsandinternationalaffairs.org/2016/the-lessons-of-effective-altruism/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180827075236/https://www.ethicsandinternationalaffairs.org/2016/the-lessons-of-effective-altruism/ |archive-date=27 August 2018 |access-date=26 August 2018 |website=Ethics & International Affairs}}</ref> [[Kelsey Piper]] argues that uncertainty is not a good reason for effective altruists to avoid acting on their best understanding of the world, because most interventions have mixed evidence regarding their effectiveness.<ref name="Piper 2022"/>

Pascal-Emmanuel Gobry and others have warned about the "[[McNamara fallacy|measurement problem]]",<ref name="Rubenstein 2016" /><ref name="Gobry 2015">{{cite magazine |last=Gobry |first=Pascal-Emmanuel |date=March 16, 2015 |title=Can Effective Altruism really change the world? |url=https://theweek.com/articles/542955/effective-altruism-really-change-world |url-status=live |magazine=[[The Week]] |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150321070005/https://theweek.com/articles/542955/effective-altruism-really-change-world |archive-date=March 21, 2015 |access-date=March 21, 2015}}</ref> with issues such as medical research or government reform worked on "one grinding step at a time", and results being hard to measure with controlled experiments. Gobry also argues that such interventions risk being undervalued by the effective altruism movement.<ref name="Gobry 2015" /> As effective altruism emphasizes a data-centric approach, critics say principles which do not lend themselves to quantification—justice, fairness, equality—get left in the sidelines.<ref name="Lewis-Kraus 2022b" /><ref name="Thompson 2015" />

===Counterfactual reasoning===
[[Counterfactual conditional|Counterfactual]] reasoning involves considering the possible outcomes of alternative choices. It has been employed by effective altruists in a number of contexts, including career choice. Many people assume that the best way to help others is through direct methods, such as working for a charity or providing social services.<ref>{{cite news |last1=Rosato |first1=Donna |last2=Wong |first2=Grace |date=November 2011 |title=Best jobs for saving the world |work=CNN |url=https://money.cnn.com/magazines/moneymag/best-jobs/2011/jobs-helping-others/1.html |url-status=live |access-date=February 28, 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130303191753/https://money.cnn.com/magazines/moneymag/best-jobs/2011/jobs-helping-others/1.html |archive-date=March 3, 2013}}</ref> However, since there is a high supply of candidates for such positions, it makes sense to compare the amount of good one candidate does to how much good the next-best candidate would do. According to this reasoning, the marginal impact of a career is likely to be smaller than the gross impact.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Todd |first1=Benjamin |title=Which Ethical Careers Make a Difference?: The Replaceability Issue in the Ethics of Career Choice |url=https://www.academia.edu/1807196 |url-status=live |language=en |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210215082734/https://www.academia.edu/1807196/Which_Ethical_Careers_Make_a_Difference_The_Replaceability_Issue_in_the_Ethics_of_Career_Choice |archive-date=February 15, 2021 |access-date=March 11, 2017 |website=University of Oxford}}</ref>

===Differences from utilitarianism===
Although EA aims for [[Utility maximization problem|maximizing]] like [[utilitarianism]], EA differs from utilitarianism in a few ways; for example, EA does not claim that people should always maximize the good [[The end justifies the means|regardless of the means]], and EA does not claim that the good is the sum total of [[well-being]].<ref name="MacAskill 2019a"/> Toby Ord has described utilitarians as "number-crunching", compared with most effective altruists whom he called "guided by conventional wisdom tempered by an eye to the numbers".<ref>{{Cite web|last=Sandbu|first=Martin|date=December 29, 2023|url=https://www.ft.com/content/128f3a15-b048-4741-b3e0-61c9346c390b|title=Effective altruism was the favoured creed of Sam Bankman-Fried. Can it survive his fall?|website=Financial Times}}</ref> Other philosophers have argued that EA still retains some core ethical commitments that are essential and distinctive to utilitarianism, such as the principle of impartiality, [[welfarism]] and good-maximization.{{Sfn|Adams|Crary|Gruen|2023|p=xxvi}}

MacAskill has argued that one should not be absolutely certain about which ethical view is correct, and that "when we are morally uncertain, we should act in a way that serves as a best compromise between different moral views".<ref name="MacAskill 2022b" /> He also wrote that even from a purely [[consequentialist]] perspective, "naive calculations that justify some harmful action because it has good consequences are, in practice, almost never correct".<ref name="MacAskill 2022b" />

=== Differences from effective accelerationism ===
[[Effective accelerationism]] (abbreviated e/acc) is influenced by ideas of [[accelerationism]]. Its proponents advocate unrestricted technological progress in the hope that [[artificial general intelligence]] will solve major challenges and maximize overall good, arguing that deceleration and stagnation of technology is a greater risk than any posed by AI. Effective altruists are generally more cautious about AI, based on their belief that going too fast could increase [[Existential risk from artificial intelligence|existential risks]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=Torres |first=Émile P. |date=2023-12-14 |title='Effective Accelerationism' and the Pursuit of Cosmic Utopia |url=https://www.truthdig.com/articles/effective-accelerationism-and-the-pursuit-of-cosmic-utopia/ |access-date=2025-02-01 |website=Truthdig |language=en-US}}</ref>

==Cause priorities==

The principles and goals of effective altruism are wide enough to support furthering any cause that allows people to do the most good, while taking into account cause neutrality.<ref name="Crouch 2013" /> B.V.E. Hyde distinguishes between the effective altruist movement (EAM) and effective altruist theory (EAT) which, he argues, have very different focusses.<ref name="Hyde 2025">{{Cite journal |last=Hyde |first=B.V.E. |date=2025-06-01 |title=Effective Altruism: Philosophical Issues Edited by Hilary Greaves and Theron Pummer |url=https://doi.org/10.1007/s10790-023-09944-7 |journal=The Journal of Value Inquiry |language=en |volume=59 |issue=2 |pages=395–399 |doi=10.1007/s10790-023-09944-7 |issn=1573-0492|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Many people in the effective altruism movement have prioritized global health and development, animal welfare, and mitigating risks that threaten the future of humanity.<ref name="Matthews 2015b" /><ref name="Skelton 2016" />

===Global health and development===
The alleviation of [[global poverty]] and [[neglected tropical diseases]] has been a focus of some of the earliest and most prominent organizations associated with effective altruism. Charity evaluator GiveWell was founded by [[Holden Karnofsky]] and [[Elie Hassenfeld]] in 2007 to address poverty,<ref>{{cite web|last1=Konduri|first1=Vimal|title=GiveWell Co-Founder Explains Effective Altruism Frameworks|url=https://www.thecrimson.com/article/2015/12/4/givewell-founder-effective-altruism/|website=The Harvard Crimson|access-date=March 10, 2017|ref=crimson|language=en|archive-date=March 13, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170313142330/https://www.thecrimson.com/article/2015/12/4/givewell-founder-effective-altruism/|url-status=live}}</ref> where they believe additional donations to be the most impactful.<ref>{{cite news|last1=Wolfe|first1=Alexandra|title=Hedge Fund Analytics for Nonprofits|url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2011-11-23/hedge-fund-analytics-for-nonprofits|access-date=March 11, 2017|work=Bloomberg.com|publisher=Bloomberg LP|date=November 24, 2011|archive-date=March 16, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170316054537/https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2011-11-23/hedge-fund-analytics-for-nonprofits|url-status=live}}</ref> GiveWell's leading recommendations include: [[malaria prevention]] charities [[Against Malaria Foundation]] and [[Malaria Consortium]], [[deworming]] charities [[Schistosomiasis Control Initiative]] and [[Deworm the World Initiative]], and [[GiveDirectly]] for direct cash transfers to beneficiaries.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.economist.com/news/international/21651815-lessons-business-charities-doing-good-doing-well|title=Doing good by doing well|newspaper=The Economist|access-date=10 March 2017|archive-date=27 December 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161227154405/https://www.economist.com/news/international/21651815-lessons-business-charities-doing-good-doing-well|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.huffingtonpost.com/2015/03/26/elie-hassenfeld-givewell_n_6927320.html|title=That Time A Hedge Funder Quit His Job And Then Raised $60 Million For Charity|last=Pitney|first=Nico|date=March 26, 2015|work=[[Huffington Post]]|access-date=April 27, 2015|archive-date=May 18, 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150518095204/https://www.huffingtonpost.com/2015/03/26/elie-hassenfeld-givewell_n_6927320.html|url-status=live}}</ref> The organization The Life You Can Save, which originated from Singer's book ''[[The Life You Can Save]]'',<ref>{{cite book |last=Singer |first=Peter |author-link=Peter Singer |date=2009 |title=[[The Life You Can Save|The Life You Can Save: Acting Now to End World Poverty]] |location=New York |publisher=Random House |isbn=9781400067107 |oclc=232980306 }}</ref> works to alleviate global poverty by promoting evidence-backed charities, conducting philanthropy education, and changing the culture of giving in affluent countries.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.huffingtonpost.com/entry/how-to-do-good-a-conversation-with-the-worlds-leading_us_58cb8dcee4b0537abd956f91|title=How To Do Good: A Conversation With The World's Leading Ethicist|last=Zhang|first=Linch|date=March 17, 2017|website=Huffington Post|language=en-US|access-date=June 1, 2017|archive-date=March 24, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170324220238/https://www.huffingtonpost.com/entry/how-to-do-good-a-conversation-with-the-worlds-leading_us_58cb8dcee4b0537abd956f91|url-status=live}}</ref>

===Animal welfare===

Improving animal welfare has been a focus of many effective altruists.<ref>{{multiref2 | {{Cite web |last=Gunther |first=Marc |title=Why the future of animal welfare lies beyond the West |work=Vox |access-date=2022-01-14 |date=2021-11-26 |url=https://www.vox.com/2021/11/26/22772693/animal-rights-welfare-movement-global-factory-farming}} | {{Cite web |last=Klein |first=Ezra |title=Peter Singer on the lives you can save |work=Vox |access-date=2022-01-14 |date=2019-12-06 |url=https://www.vox.com/future-perfect/2019/12/6/20992100/peter-singer-effective-altruism-lives-you-can-save-animal-liberation}} | {{Cite web |last=Matthews |first=Dylan |title=The wild frontier of animal welfare |work=Vox |access-date=2022-01-14 |date=2021-04-12 |url=https://www.vox.com/the-highlight/22325435/animal-welfare-wild-animals-movement}} }}</ref> Singer and [[Animal Charity Evaluators]] (ACE) have argued that effective altruists should prioritize changes to factory farming over pet welfare.<ref name="Kristof 2015" /> Over 80 billion land animals are [[Animal slaughter|slaughtered]] and between 1 and 2.7 trillion individual fish are killed each year for human consumption.<ref>{{multiref2 | {{Cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/commentisfree/cif-green/2010/sep/14/fish-forgotten-victims|title=Fish: the forgotten victims on our plate|date=2010-09-14|work=The Guardian|access-date=June 14, 2017|language=en-GB|issn=0261-3077|archive-date=2017-08-01|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170801072456/https://www.theguardian.com/commentisfree/cif-green/2010/sep/14/fish-forgotten-victims|url-status=live}} | {{Cite book|url=http://www.fao.org/fileadmin/user_upload/animalwelfare/GlobalWarningExecutiveSummary1.pdf|title=Global Warming Climate Change and Farm Animal Welfare|publisher=[[Compassion in World Farming]]|year=2008|access-date=2017-06-14|archive-date=2016-05-27|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160527193033/http://www.fao.org/fileadmin/user_upload/animalwelfare/GlobalWarningExecutiveSummary1.pdf|url-status=dead}} | {{Cite book|url=http://www.fishcount.org.uk/published/standard/fishcountfullrptSR.pdf|title=Worse things happen at sea: the welfare of wild-caught fish|last=Mood|first=Alison|publisher=fishcount.org.uk|year=2010|access-date=2017-06-14|archive-date=2017-05-19|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170519182157/http://www.fishcount.org.uk/published/standard/fishcountfullrptSR.pdf|url-status=live}} }}</ref> A number of non-profit organizations have been established that adopt an effective altruist approach toward animal welfare. ACE evaluates animal charities based on their cost-effectiveness and transparency, particularly those tackling factory farming.<ref name="Singer 2015" />{{rp|139}}<ref>{{harvnb|Piper|2018a}}; {{Cite web |last=Engber |first=Daniel |date=2016-08-18 |title=How the Chicken Became the Unlikely Focus of the Animal Rights Movement |url=https://slate.com/technology/2016/08/animal-activists-crunched-the-numbers-to-learn-that-the-creature-most-in-need-of-their-support-was-the-lowly-chicken.html |access-date=2022-08-13 |website=Slate Magazine |language=en}}</ref> [[Faunalytics]] focuses on animal welfare research.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.vox.com/future-perfect/2018/10/31/18026418/vegan-vegetarian-animal-welfare-corporate-advocacy|title=Vegan diets are hard to sell. Animal activists might do better focused on corporate decisions, not people's plates.|last=Piper|first=Kelsey|date=2018-10-31|website=Vox|access-date=2019-01-12|archive-date=2019-01-13|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190113232119/https://www.vox.com/future-perfect/2018/10/31/18026418/vegan-vegetarian-animal-welfare-corporate-advocacy|url-status=live}}</ref>  Other animal initiatives affiliated with effective altruism include [[Animal Ethics (organization)|Animal Ethics]]' and [[Wild Animal Initiative]]'s work on [[wild animal suffering]],<ref>{{Cite web|last=Matthews|first=Dylan|date=2021-04-12|title=The wild frontier of animal welfare|url=https://www.vox.com/the-highlight/22325435/animal-welfare-wild-animals-movement|access-date=2021-09-05|website=Vox|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|title="Effective Altruism for Animals" Panel, Animal Studies|url=http://animalstudies.as.nyu.edu/object/as.events.20150729|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170312085320/http://animalstudies.as.nyu.edu/object/as.events.20150729|archive-date=12 March 2017|access-date=11 March 2017|website=New York University Animal Studies Initiative|publisher=NYU}}</ref> addressing farm animal suffering with [[cultured meat]],<ref>{{multiref2 | {{Cite web |title=How one founder aims to bring researchers and food producers together around cultured meat |work=TechCrunch |date=August 23, 2021 |access-date=2022-01-14 |url=https://techcrunch.com/podcast/how-one-founder-aims-to-bring-researchers-and-food-producers-together-around-cultured-meat/}} | {{Cite web |date=2021-11-09 |title=Is Anyone Right About the Future of Cultivated Meat? Does It Matter? |url=https://www.greenqueen.com.hk/cultivated-meat-future/ |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211109230424/https://www.greenqueen.com.hk/cultivated-meat-future/ |archive-date=2021-11-09 |access-date=2022-01-14 |work=Green Queen}} }}</ref> and increasing concern for all kinds of animals.<ref name="Torrella 2021" /><ref>{{multiref2 | {{Cite news |last=Lombrozo |first=Tania |title=Expanding The Circle Of Moral Concern |work=NPR |access-date=2022-01-14 |date=2016-11-15 |url=https://www.npr.org/sections/13.7/2016/11/15/501972594/expanding-the-circle-of-moral-concern}} | {{Cite web |last=Samuel |first=Sigal |title=Should animals, plants, and robots have the same rights as you? |work=Vox |access-date=2022-01-14 |date=2019-04-04 |url=https://www.vox.com/future-perfect/2019/4/4/18285986/robot-animal-nature-expanding-moral-circle-peter-singer}} }}</ref> The [[Sentience Institute]] is a [[think tank]] founded to [[Moral circle expansion|expand the moral circle]] to other [[Sentience|sentient]] beings.<ref>{{cite web |last=Sigal |first=Samuel |date=2019-04-04 |title=Moral circle expansion: should animals, plants, and robots have the same rights as humans? |url=https://www.vox.com/future-perfect/2019/4/4/18285986/robot-animal-nature-expanding-moral-circle-peter-singer |website=Vox |access-date=2021-12-01}}</ref>

===Long-term future and global catastrophic risks===

The ethical stance of [[longtermism]], emphasizing the importance of positively influencing the long-term future, developed closely in relation to effective altruism.<ref>{{Cite web |last=MacAskill |first=William |date=2022-08-07 |title=What is longtermism? |url=https://www.bbc.com/future/article/20220805-what-is-longtermism-and-why-does-it-matter |access-date=2022-09-15 |website=[[BBC]] |language=en}}</ref><ref name="MacAskill 2022a">{{Cite news |last=MacAskill |first=William |date=2022-08-05 |title=Opinion {{!}} The Case for Longtermism |language=en-US |work=The New York Times |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2022/08/05/opinion/the-case-for-longtermism.html |access-date=2022-09-15 |issn=0362-4331}}</ref> Longtermism argues that "distance in time is like distance in space", suggesting that the welfare of future individuals matters as much as the welfare of currently existing individuals. Given the potentially extremely high number of individuals that could exist in the future, longtermists seek to decrease the probability that an existential catastrophe irreversibly ruins it.<ref>{{Cite news |title=What is long-termism? |url=https://www.economist.com/the-economist-explains/2022/11/22/what-is-long-termism |access-date=2024-05-16 |newspaper=The Economist |issn=0013-0613}}</ref> Toby Ord has stated that "the people of the future may be even more powerless to protect themselves from the risks we impose than the dispossessed of our own time".<ref>{{cite book |last=Ord |first=Toby |title=[[The Precipice: Existential Risk and the Future of Humanity]] |date=2020 |publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing |isbn=9781526600196 |location=London |chapter=Introduction |oclc=1143365836 |author-link=Toby Ord |access-date=2020-04-06 |chapter-url=https://cdn.80000hours.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/03/The-Precipice-Introduction-Chapter-1.pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200406050608/https://cdn.80000hours.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/03/The-Precipice-Introduction-Chapter-1.pdf |archive-date=2020-04-06 |url-status=live}}</ref>{{rp|8}}

[[Existential risk]]s, such as dangers associated with [[biotechnology]] and [[Existential risk from advanced artificial intelligence|advanced artificial intelligence]], are often highlighted and the subject of active research.<ref name="MacAskill 2022a"/> Existential risks have such huge impacts that achieving a very small change in such a risk—say a 0.0001-percent reduction—"might be worth more than saving a billion people today", reported Gideon Lewis-Kraus in 2022, but he added that nobody in the EA community openly endorses such an extreme conclusion.<ref name="Lewis-Kraus 2022b" />

Organizations devoted to working on research and advocacy for improving the long-term future, and connected to the effective altruism community, include the [[Centre for the Study of Existential Risk]] at the University of Cambridge, the [[Future of Life Institute]], and the now-defunct [[Future of Humanity Institute]] at the University of Oxford.<ref>{{cite news|last1=Guan|first1=Melody|title=The New Social Movement of our Generation: Effective Altruism|url=https://harvardpolitics.com/harvard/new-social-movement-generation-effective-altruism/|access-date=11 March 2017|work=Harvard Political Review|date=19 April 2015|archive-date=12 March 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170312073154/https://harvardpolitics.com/harvard/new-social-movement-generation-effective-altruism/|url-status=live}}</ref> In addition, the [[Machine Intelligence Research Institute]] is focused on the more narrow mission of [[Existential risk from artificial general intelligence#Mitigation|managing advanced artificial intelligence]].<ref>{{multiref2 | {{Cite web|url=https://www.vox.com/future-perfect/2018/12/21/18126576/ai-artificial-intelligence-machine-learning-safety-alignment|title=The case for taking AI seriously as a threat to humanity|last=Piper|first=Kelsey|date=2018-12-21|website=Vox|access-date=2019-01-12|archive-date=2019-01-13|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190113062721/https://www.vox.com/future-perfect/2018/12/21/18126576/ai-artificial-intelligence-machine-learning-safety-alignment|url-status=live}} | {{cite news|last1=Basulto|first1=Dominic|title=The very best ideas for preventing artificial intelligence from wrecking the planet|url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/innovations/wp/2015/07/07/the-very-best-ideas-for-preventing-artificial-intelligence-from-wrecking-the-planet/|access-date=March 11, 2017|newspaper=The Washington Post|date=July 7, 2015|archive-date=March 16, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170316074301/https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/innovations/wp/2015/07/07/the-very-best-ideas-for-preventing-artificial-intelligence-from-wrecking-the-planet/|url-status=live}} }}</ref>

==== S-risks ====
{{Main|Risk of astronomical suffering}}

Some effective altruists focus on reducing risks of astronomical suffering (s-risks). S-risks are a particularly severe type of existential risk due to their potential scope and severity, surpassing even human extinction in negative impact. Efforts to mitigate these risks include research and advocacy by organizations like the Center on Long-Term Risk, which explores strategies to avoid large-scale suffering. S-risks could arise from a long-term neglect for the welfare of some types of sentient beings. Another suggested scenario involves repressive totalitarian regimes that would become irreversibly stable due to advanced technology.<ref>{{Cite web |date=16 October 2020 |title=The grim fate that could be 'worse than extinction' |url=https://www.bbc.com/future/article/20201014-totalitarian-world-in-chains-artificial-intelligence |access-date=2025-01-03 |website=BBC |language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=Introduction to Research |url=https://centerforreducingsuffering.org/research/intro/ |website=Center for Reducing Suffering |accessdate=2024-12-31}}</ref>

== Approaches ==
Effective altruists pursue different approaches to doing good, such as donating to effective charitable organizations, using their career to make more money for donations or directly contributing their labor, and starting new non-profit or for-profit ventures.

=== Donation ===

====Financial donation====
Many effective altruists engage in charitable [[donation]]. Some believe it is a moral duty to alleviate [[suffering]] through donations if other possible uses of those funds do not offer comparable benefits to oneself.<ref name="Singer 1972" /> Some lead a [[frugality|frugal lifestyle]] in order to donate more.<ref>"Living modestly to give more", in {{harvnb|Singer|2015|pp=23–37}}; {{cite news |last1=Burton |first1=Paul |date=October 13, 2015 |title=Family Gives Away Half Their Income To Help Others |work=[[WBZ-TV]] |url=https://boston.cbslocal.com/2015/10/13/family-gives-away-half-their-income-to-help-others/ |url-status=live |access-date=March 11, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170930185608/https://boston.cbslocal.com/2015/10/13/family-gives-away-half-their-income-to-help-others/ |archive-date=September 30, 2017}}</ref> Giving What We Can (GWWC) is an organization whose members pledge to donate at least 10% of their future income to the causes that they believe are the most effective. GWWC was founded in 2009 by Toby Ord, who lives on £18,000 ($27,000) per year and donates the balance of his income.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/magazine-11950843|title=Toby Ord: Why I'm giving £1m to charity|last=Geoghegan|first=Tom|date=December 13, 2010|access-date=March 2, 2013|newspaper=BBC News|archive-date=May 13, 2013|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130513221027/https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/magazine-11950843|url-status=live}}</ref> In 2020, Ord said that people had donated over $100 million to date through the GWWC pledge.<ref>{{cite web |last=Matthews |first=Dylan |date=30 November 2020 |title=Toby Ord explains his pledge to give 10% of his pay to charity |url=https://www.vox.com/future-perfect/21728925/charity-10-percent-tithe-giving-what-we-can-toby-ord |website=[[Vox (website)|Vox]] |access-date=2021-12-06}}</ref> [[Founders Pledge]] is a similar initiative, founded out of the non-profit Founders Forum for Good, whereby entrepreneurs make a legally binding commitment to donate a percentage of their personal proceeds to charity in the event that they sell their business.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Shead |first=Sam |title=Facebook's little-known billionaire cofounder is funding a London charity |url=https://www.businessinsider.com/facebook-cofounder-dustin-moskovitz-is-funding-founders-pledge-2017-3 |access-date=2024-05-12 |website=Business Insider |language=en-US}}</ref> As of April 2024, nearly 1,900 entrepreneurs had pledged around $10 billion and nearly $1.1 billion had been donated.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Home |url=https://founderspledge.com/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240405170713/https://www.founderspledge.com/ |archive-date=2024-04-05 |access-date=2023-04-12 |website=Founders Pledge}}</ref>

====Organ donation====
EA has been used to argue that humans should [[organ donation|donate organ]]s, whilst alive or after death, and some effective altruists do.<ref>{{harvnb|Singer|2015|pp=4, 14, 67–72, 100–101}}; {{cite journal|title=Effective altruists ought to be allowed to sell their kidneys|first=Ryan|last=Tonkens|date=March 2018|journal=[[Bioethics (journal)|Bioethics]]|volume=32|issue=3|pages=147–154|doi=10.1111/bioe.12427|pmid=29369383}}</ref>

===Career choice===

Effective altruists often consider using their career to do good,<ref>{{cite web|last1=Oliver|first1=Huw|title='Effective altruists' are a new type of nice person|url=https://www.vice.com/en/article/why-are-young-people-being-so-altruistic-209/|website=[[Vice (magazine)|Vice]]|date=6 October 2014|access-date=March 11, 2017|language=en-us|archive-date=12 March 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170312090615/https://www.vice.com/en_uk/article/why-are-young-people-being-so-altruistic-209|url-status=live}}</ref> both by direct service and indirectly through their consumption, investment, and donation decisions.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=William|first1=MacAskill|title=Replaceability, Career Choice, and Making a Difference|journal=Ethical Theory and Moral Practice|volume=17|issue=2|pages=269–283|date=2014|doi=10.1007/s10677-013-9433-4|s2cid=143054318|url=https://www.academia.edu/1557895|language=en|issn=1386-2820|access-date=2017-03-11|archive-date=2021-02-15|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210215082740/https://www.academia.edu/1557895/Replaceability_Career_Choice_and_Making_a_Difference|url-status=live}}</ref> 80,000 Hours is an organization that conducts research and gives advice on which careers have the largest positive impact.{{sfn|Matthews|2018a}}<ref>{{cite news|url = https://techcrunch.com/2015/08/04/80000-hours/|title = Want To Make An Impact With Your Work? Try Some Advice From 80,000 Hours|date = August 4, 2015|access-date = October 31, 2015|work = [[TechCrunch]]|archive-date = November 9, 2015|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20151109222847/https://techcrunch.com/2015/08/04/80000-hours/|url-status = live}}</ref>

==== Earning to give ====
{{Excerpt|Earning to give}}

=== Founding effective organizations ===

Some effective altruists start non-profit or for-profit organizations to implement [[cost-effective]] ways of doing good. On the non-profit side, for example, [[Michael Kremer]] and [[Rachel Glennerster]] conducted [[randomized controlled trial]]s in Kenya to find out the best way to improve students' test scores. They tried new textbooks and flip charts, as well as smaller class sizes, but found that the only intervention that raised school attendance was treating intestinal worms in children. Based on their findings, they started the [[Deworm the World Initiative]].<ref name="Thompson 2015" /> From 2013 to August 2022, GiveWell designated Deworm the World (now run by nonprofit [[Evidence Action]]) as a top charity based on their assessment that [[mass deworming]] is "generally highly cost-effective";<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.givewell.org/charities/deworm-world-initiative/August-2022-version|title=Evidence Action's Deworm the World Initiative – August 2022 version|website=GiveWell|date=August 2022|access-date=2022-11-06}}</ref> however, there is substantial uncertainty about the benefits of mass deworming programs, with some studies finding long-term effects and others not.<ref name="Piper 2022">{{cite web|url=https://www.vox.com/future-perfect/2022/7/19/23268786/deworming-givewell-effective-altruism-michael-hobbes|title=The return of the "worm wars"|last=Piper|first=Kelsey|author-link=Kelsey Piper|website=Vox|date=2022-07-19|access-date=2022-11-06}}</ref> The Happier Lives Institute conducts research on the effectiveness of [[cognitive behavioral therapy]] (CBT) in developing countries;<ref>{{Cite web| last = Matthews| first = Dylan| title = Is therapy the best way to make the world happier?| work = Vox| access-date = 2021-12-28| date = 2021-11-18| url = https://www.vox.com/future-perfect/22765423/cbt-therapy-developing-world-strongminds}}</ref> Canopie develops an app that provides cognitive behavioural therapy to women who are expecting or postpartum;<ref>{{Cite web| title = Mayor Bowser Announces Partnership to Provide Free Access to the Canopie Maternal Mental Health Program {{!}} mayormb| access-date = 2021-12-28| url = https://mayor.dc.gov/release/mayor-bowser-announces-partnership-provide-free-access-canopie-maternal-mental-health |website=mayor.dc.gov |date=September 15, 2021}}</ref> Giving Green analyzes and ranks climate interventions for effectiveness;<ref>{{multiref2 | {{Cite web |last=Meyer |first=Robinson |title=The Best Way to Donate to Fight Climate Change (Probably) |work=The Atlantic |access-date=2021-12-28 |date=2020-12-01 |url=https://www.theatlantic.com/science/archive/2020/12/how-to-donate-to-fight-climate-change-effectively/617248/}} | {{Cite web |last=Samuel |first=Sigal |title=Want to fight climate change effectively? Here's where to donate your money. |work=Vox |access-date=2021-12-28 |date=2019-12-02 |url=https://www.vox.com/future-perfect/2019/12/2/20976180/climate-change-best-charities-effective-philanthropy}} }}</ref> the Fish Welfare Initiative works on improving animal welfare in fishing and aquaculture;<ref name="Torrella 2021">{{Cite web| last = Torrella| first = Kenny| title = The next frontier for animal welfare: Fish| work = Vox| access-date = 2021-12-28| date = 2021-03-02| url = https://www.vox.com/future-perfect/22301931/fish-animal-welfare-plant-based}}</ref> and the [[Lead Exposure Elimination Project]] works on reducing [[lead poisoning]] in developing countries.<ref>{{Cite web| last = Matthews| first = Dylan| title = Nearly half the world's kids are exposed to dangerous levels of lead| work = Vox| access-date = 2022-01-14| date = 2022-01-14| url = https://www.vox.com/future-perfect/22834666/lead-exposure-poisoning-developing-countries}}</ref>

=== Incremental versus systemic change ===
While much of the initial focus of effective altruism was on direct strategies such as health interventions and cash transfers, more [[Structural fix|systematic]] social, economic, and political reforms have also attracted attention.<ref>{{cite web|last1=Weathers|first1=Scott|title=Can 'effective altruism' change the world? It already has.|url=https://www.opendemocracy.net/transformation/scott-weathers/can-effective-altruism-change-world-it-already-has|website=Transformation|access-date=11 March 2017|language=en|date=29 February 2016|archive-date=12 March 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170312135844/https://www.opendemocracy.net/transformation/scott-weathers/can-effective-altruism-change-world-it-already-has|url-status=live}}</ref> Mathew Snow in ''[[Jacobin (magazine)|Jacobin]]'' wrote that effective altruism "implores individuals to use their money to procure necessities for those who desperately need them, but says nothing about the system that determines how those necessities are produced and distributed in the first place".<ref>{{cite web |last=Snow |first=Mathew |date=August 25, 2015 |title=Against Charity |url=https://www.jacobinmag.com/2015/08/peter-singer-charity-effective-altruism/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150828121204/https://www.jacobinmag.com/2015/08/peter-singer-charity-effective-altruism/ |archive-date=August 28, 2015 |access-date=September 5, 2016 |website=[[Jacobin (magazine)|Jacobin]]}}</ref> Philosopher [[Amia Srinivasan]] criticized William MacAskill's ''[[Doing Good Better]]'' for a perceived lack of coverage of [[global inequality]] and
