[[oppression]], while noting that effective altruism is in principle open to whichever means of doing good is most effective, including political advocacy aimed at systemic change.<ref name="Srinivasan 2015">{{cite journal|url=https://www.lrb.co.uk/the-paper/v37/n18/amia-srinivasan/stop-the-robot-apocalypse|title=Stop the Robot Apocalypse|last=Srinivasan|first=Amia|author-link=Amia Srinivasan|journal=London Review of Books|date=24 September 2015|volume=37 |issue=18 |access-date=20 September 2022}}</ref> Srinivasan said, "Effective altruism has so far been a rather homogeneous movement of middle-class white men fighting poverty through largely conventional means, but it is at least in theory a broad church."<ref name="Srinivasan 2015"/> [[Judith Lichtenberg]] in ''[[The New Republic]]'' said that effective altruists "neglect the kind of structural and political change that is ultimately necessary".{{sfn|Lichtenberg|2015}} An article in ''[[The Ecologist]]'' published in 2016 argued that effective altruism is an apolitical attempt to solve political problems, describing the concept as "pseudo-scientific".<ref>{{cite news |url=https://theecologist.org/2016/apr/05/why-effective-altruism-ineffective-case-refugees |title=Why 'Effective Altruism' is ineffective: the case of refugees |work=The Ecologist |last1=Earle |first1=Sam |last2=Read |first2=Rupert |date=5 April 2016 |access-date=23 November 2022}}</ref> The Ethiopian-American AI scientist [[Timnit Gebru]] has condemned effective altruists "for acting as though their concerns are above structural issues as racism and colonialism", as Gideon Lewis-Kraus summarized her views in 2022.<ref name="Lewis-Kraus 2022b" />

Philosophers such as Susan Dwyer, Joshua Stein, and [[Olúfẹ́mi O. Táíwò]] have criticized effective altruism for furthering the disproportionate influence of wealthy individuals in domains that should be the responsibility of democratic governments and organizations.<ref>{{multiref2 | {{cite news |last=Dwyer |first=Susan |date=23 January 2015 |title=Altruism can be all too effective |work=Al Jazeera America |url=http://america.aljazeera.com/opinions/2015/1/altruism-can-be-all-too-effective.html |access-date=23 November 2022}} | {{cite news |last1=Táíwò |first1=Olúfẹ́mi O. |last2=Stein |first2=Joshua |date=16 November 2022 |title=Is the effective altruism movement in trouble? |work=The Guardian |url=https://www.theguardian.com/commentisfree/2022/nov/16/is-the-effective-altruism-movement-in-trouble |access-date=23 November 2022}} }}</ref> Arguments have been made that movements focused on systemic or institutional change, for example [[democratization]], are compatible with effective altruism.<ref>{{cite journal |last=Kissel |first=Joshua |date=January 31, 2017 |title=Effective Altruism and Anti-Capitalism: An Attempt at Reconciliation |journal=Essays in Philosophy |volume=18 |issue=1 |pages=68–90 |doi=10.7710/1526-0569.1573 |doi-access=free}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Berkey |first1=Brian |date=2018 |title=The Institutional Critique of Effective Altruism |url=https://faculty.wharton.upenn.edu/wp-content/uploads/2017/06/The-Institutional-Critique-of-Effective-Altruism.pdf |url-status=live |journal=Utilitas |volume=30 |issue=2 |pages=143–171 |doi=10.1017/S0953820817000176 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170913230116/https://faculty.wharton.upenn.edu/wp-content/uploads/2017/06/The-Institutional-Critique-of-Effective-Altruism.pdf |archive-date=13 September 2017 |access-date=26 August 2018 |s2cid=12014675}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last=Syme |first=Timothy |date=February 2019 |title=Charity vs. revolution: effective altruism and the systemic change objection |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/330947080 |journal=[[Ethical Theory and Moral Practice]] |volume=22 |issue=1 |pages=93–120 |doi=10.1007/s10677-019-09979-5 |s2cid=150872907}}</ref> Philosopher Elizabeth Ashford posits that people are obligated to both donate to effective aid charities and to [[Structural change|reform the structures]] that are responsible for poverty.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Ashford |first1=Elizabeth |title=The Ethics of Giving: Philosophers' Perspectives on Philanthropy |date=2018 |publisher=[[Oxford University Press]] |isbn=9780190648879 |editor1-last=Woodruff |editor1-first=Paul |location=Oxford; New York |pages=103–148 |chapter=Severe Poverty as an Unjust Emergency |volume=1 |doi=10.1093/oso/9780190648879.003.0005 |oclc=1025376469}}</ref> Open Philanthropy has given grants for progressive advocacy work in areas such as criminal justice,<ref name="Matthews 2015b" /><ref>{{Cite news |last=Schoffstall |first=Joe |date=2 August 2021 |title=Mark Zuckerberg cash discreetly leaked into far-left prosecutor races |website=[[Fox News]] |url=https://www.foxnews.com/politics/mark-zuckerberg-campaign-donations-progressive-prosecutors |access-date=2022-02-06}}</ref> economic stabilization,<ref name="Matthews 2015b" /> and housing reform,<ref>{{multiref2 | {{Cite magazine |last=Bronstein |first=Zelda |date=September–October 2018 |title=California's 'Yimbys': The Growth Machine's Shock Troops |url=https://dollarsandsense.org/archives/2018/0918bronstein.html |magazine=[[Dollars & Sense]] |access-date=2022-02-06}} | {{Cite web |last=Redmond |first=Tim |date=26 May 2021 |title=The big Yimby money behind housing deregulation bills |url=https://48hills.org/2021/05/the-big-yimby-money-behind-housing-deregulation-bills/ |website=48hills.org |access-date=2022-02-06}} }}</ref> despite pegging the success of political reform as being "highly uncertain".<ref name="Matthews 2015b" />

== Psychological research ==
{{Main|Psychological barriers to effective altruism}}
Researchers in [[psychology]] and related fields have identified psychological barriers to effective altruism that can cause people to choose other options when they engage in altruistic activities such as charitable giving.<ref>{{multiref2 | {{cite book |last1=Baron |first1=Jonathan |author-link1=Jonathan Baron |last2=Szymanska |first2=Ewa |chapter=Heuristics and Biases in Charity |date=2011 |editor1-last=Oppenheimer |editor1-first=Daniel M. |editor-link1=Daniel M. Oppenheimer |editor2-last=Olivola |editor2-first=Christopher Yves |title=The Science of Giving: Experimental Approaches to the Study of Charity |pages=215–235 |series=The Society for Judgment and Decision Making series |location=New York |publisher=Psychology Press |doi=10.4324/9780203865972-24 |isbn=9781138981430 |oclc=449889661}} | {{Cite journal |last1=Burum |first1=Bethany |last2=Nowak |first2=Martin A. |last3=Hoffman |first3=Moshe |date=December 2020 |title=An evolutionary explanation for ineffective altruism |url=https://www.nature.com/articles/s41562-020-00950-4 |journal=Nature Human Behaviour |language=en |volume=4 |issue=12 |pages=1245–1257 |doi=10.1038/s41562-020-00950-4 |pmid=33046859 |s2cid=222318993 |issn=2397-3374|url-access=subscription }} | {{Cite journal |last1=Caviola |first1=Lucius |last2=Schubert |first2=Stefan |last3=Greene |first3=Joshua D. |date=July 2021 |title=The Psychology of (In)Effective Altruism |journal=Trends in Cognitive Sciences |volume=25 |issue=7 |pages=596–607 |doi=10.1016/j.tics.2021.03.015 |pmid=33962844 |issn=1364-6613|doi-access=free }} | {{Cite journal |last1=Jaeger |first1=Bastian |last2=van Vugt |first2=Mark |date=April 2022 |title=Psychological barriers to effective altruism: An evolutionary perspective |journal=Current Opinion in Psychology |volume=44 |pages=130–134 |doi=10.1016/j.copsyc.2021.09.008 |pmid=34628365 |s2cid=238582556 |doi-access=free }} }}</ref>

== Other criticism and controversies ==
Although the movement's original leaders were associated with frugal lifestyles, the arrival of big donors, including Bankman-Fried, led to more spending and opulence, which seemed incongruous with the movement's espoused values.<ref name="Lewis-Kraus 2022a" /><ref name="Wenar 2024" /> In 2022, Effective Ventures Foundation purchased the estate of [[Wytham Abbey]] for the purpose of running workshops,<ref name="Lewis-Kraus 2022b" /> but put it up for sale in 2024.<ref>{{cite news |last=Neate |first=Rupert |date=12 May 2024 |title=Wytham Abbey put up for sale for £15m by effective altruism group EVF |newspaper=The Guardian |url=https://www.theguardian.com/business/article/2024/may/12/wytham-abbey-sale-effective-altruism-group-evf}}</ref>

[[Timnit Gebru]] claimed that effective altruism has acted to overrule any other concerns regarding [[AI ethics]] (e.g. [[Deepfake pornography|deepfake porn]], [[algorithmic bias]]), in the name of either preventing or [[AI alignment|controlling]] [[artificial general intelligence]].<ref>{{Cite magazine |last=Gebru |first=Timnit |title=Effective Altruism Is Pushing a Dangerous Brand of 'AI Safety' |url=https://www.wired.com/story/effective-altruism-artificial-intelligence-sam-bankman-fried/ |access-date=2024-06-22 |magazine=Wired |language=en-US |issn=1059-1028}}</ref> She and [[Émile P. Torres]] further assert that the movement belongs to a network of interconnected movements they've termed [[TESCREAL]], which they contend serves as intellectual justification for wealthy donors to shape humanity's future.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Gebru |first1=Timnit |last2=Torres |first2=Émile P. |date=2024-04-14 |title=The TESCREAL bundle: Eugenics and the promise of utopia through artificial general intelligence |url=https://firstmonday.org/ojs/index.php/fm/article/view/13636 |journal=[[First Monday (journal)|First Monday]] |language=en |doi=10.5210/fm.v29i4.13636 |doi-access=free |issn=1396-0466}}</ref>

=== Sam Bankman-Fried ===
[[Sam Bankman-Fried]], the eventual founder of the [[cryptocurrency exchange]] [[FTX (company)|FTX]], had a lunch with philosopher [[William MacAskill]] in 2012 while he was an undergraduate at MIT in which MacAskill encouraged him to go earn money and donate it, rather than volunteering his time for causes.<ref name="Tiku 2022">{{cite news |last=Tiku |first=Nitasha |date=2022-11-17 |title=The do-gooder movement that shielded Sam Bankman-Fried from scrutiny |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/technology/2022/11/17/effective-altruism-sam-bankman-fried-ftx-crypto/ |access-date=2022-11-25 |newspaper=The Washington Post |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name="Kulish 2022">{{Cite news |last=Kulish |first=Nicholas |date=October 18, 2022 |title=How a Scottish Moral Philosopher Got Elon Musk's Number |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2022/10/08/business/effective-altruism-elon-musk.html |access-date=November 24, 2023 |work=The New York Times}}</ref> Bankman-Fried went on to a career in investing and around 2019 became more publicly associated with the effective altruism movement,<ref>{{Cite news |last=Osipovich |first=Alexander |date=April 16, 2021 |title=This Vegan Billionaire Disrupted the Crypto Markets. Stocks May Be Next. |url=https://www.wsj.com/articles/this-vegan-billionaire-disrupted-the-crypto-markets-stocks-may-be-next-11618565408 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210604162221/https://www.wsj.com/articles/this-vegan-billionaire-disrupted-the-crypto-markets-stocks-may-be-next-11618565408 |archive-date=June 4, 2021 |newspaper=[[The Wall Street Journal]]}}</ref> announcing that his goal was to "donate as much as [he] can".<ref>{{Cite web |last=Schleifer |first=Theodore |date=March 20, 2021 |title=How a crypto billionaire decided to become one of Biden's biggest donors |url=https://www.vox.com/recode/2021/3/20/22335209/sam-bankman-fried-joe-biden-ftx-cryptocurrency-effective-altruism |website=Vox}}</ref> Bankman-Fried founded the FTX Future Fund, which brought on MacAskill as one of its advisers, and which made a $13.9 million grant to the [[Centre for Effective Altruism]] where MacAskill holds a board role.<ref name="Kulish 2022" />

After [[bankruptcy of FTX|the collapse of FTX]] in late 2022, the movement underwent additional public scrutiny.<ref name="Wenar 2024">{{Cite magazine |last=Wenar |first=Leif |author-link=Leif Wenar |title=The Deaths of Effective Altruism |url=https://www.wired.com/story/deaths-of-effective-altruism/ |access-date=2024-06-27 |magazine=[[Wired (magazine)|Wired]] |language=en-US |issn=1059-1028 |url-access=subscription}}</ref><ref name="Samuel 2022" /><ref name="Tiku 2022" /><ref>{{cite news |last=Hiltzik |first=Michael |author-link=Michael Hiltzik |date=2022-11-21 |title=Column: How Sam Bankman-Fried exploited the 'effective altruism' fad to get rich and con the world |url=https://www.latimes.com/business/story/2022-11-21/column-how-sam-bankman-fried-exploited-the-fad-of-effective-altruism-to-get-rich-and-con-the-world |access-date=2022-11-23 |work=Los Angeles Times |language=en-US}}</ref> Bankman-Fried's relationship with effective altruism damaged the movement's reputation.<ref name="Kulish 2022" /><ref>{{multiref2 | {{Cite web |last=Lowrey |first=Annie |date=2022-11-17 |title=Effective Altruism Committed the Sin It Was Supposed to Correct |url=https://www.theatlantic.com/ideas/archive/2022/11/cryptocurrency-effective-altruism-ftx-sam-bankman-fried/672149/ |access-date=2022-11-21 |website=[[The Atlantic]] |language=en}} | {{Cite news |date=2022-11-17 |title=What Sam Bankman-Fried's downfall means for effective altruism |url=https://www.economist.com/briefing/2022/11/17/what-sam-bankman-frieds-downfall-means-for-effective-altruism |access-date=2022-11-21 |newspaper=The Economist |issn=0013-0613}} | {{cite web |last=Hannah |first=Jonathan |date=November 18, 2022 |title=Sam Bankman-Fried's downfall is more than a black eye for Effective Altruism |url=https://philanthropydaily.com/sam-bankman-frieds-downfall-is-more-than-a-black-eye-for-effective-altruism/ |website=Philanthropy Daily}} }}</ref> Some journalists asked whether the effective altruist movement was complicit in FTX's collapse, because it was convenient for leaders to overlook specific warnings about Bankman-Fried's behavior or questionable ethics at the trading firm Alameda.<ref name="Lewis-Kraus 2022a">{{cite magazine |last=Lewis-Kraus |first=Gideon |date=December 1, 2022 |title=Sam Bankman-Fried, Effective Altruism and the Question of Complicity |url=https://www.newyorker.com/news/annals-of-inquiry/sam-bankman-fried-effective-altruism-and-the-question-of-complicity |magazine=[[The New Yorker]]}}</ref><ref>{{cite magazine |last=Levitz |first=Eric |date=November 16, 2022 |title=Is Effective Altruism to Blame for Sam Bankman-Fried? |url=https://nymag.com/intelligencer/2022/11/effective-altruism-sam-bankman-fried-sbf-ftx-crypto.html |magazine=[[New York (magazine)|New York]]}}</ref> ''[[Fortune (magazine)|Fortune]]'''s crypto editor Jeff John Roberts said that "Bankman-Fried and his cronies professed devotion to 'EA,' but all their high-minded words turned out to be flimflam to justify robbing people".<ref name="Roberts 2023">{{cite magazine |last=Roberts |first=Jeff John |date=November 20, 2023 |title=OpenAI's crackup is another black eye for effective altruism |url=https://fortune.com/crypto/2023/11/20/openai-sam-altman-ousting-effective-altruism/ |magazine=[[Fortune (magazine)|Fortune]]}}</ref> MacAskill condemned Bankman-Fried's actions, saying that effective altruism emphasizes integrity.<ref name="Samuel 2022">{{cite news |last=Samuel |first=Sigal |date=16 November 2022 |title=Effective altruism gave rise to Sam Bankman-Fried. Now it's facing a moral reckoning. |url=https://www.vox.com/future-perfect/23458282/effective-altruism-sam-bankman-fried-ftx-crypto-ethics |access-date=19 November 2022 |website=Vox}}</ref><ref>{{cite tweet |number=1591218030364995585 |user=willmacaskill |title=A clear-thinking EA should strongly oppose "ends justify the means" reasoning. I hope to write more soon about this. In the meantime, here are some links to writings produced over the years. |first=William |last=MacAskill |author-link=William MacAskill |date=11 November 2022 |access-date=2 December 2022 |language=en |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221124192932/https://twitter.com/willmacaskill/status/1591218030364995585 |archive-date=24 November 2022 |url-status=live}}</ref> Philosopher [[Leif Wenar]] argued that Bankman-Fried's conduct typified much of the movement by focusing on positive impacts and [[expected value]] without adequately weighing [[Philanthropy#Criticism|risk and harm from philanthropy]]. He argued that the FTX case is not separable, as some in the EA community maintained, from the assumptions and reasoning that molded effective altruism as a philosophy in the first place—assumptions and reasoning that Wenar considered to be simplistic.<ref name="Wenar 2024" />

=== Sexual misconduct accusations ===
Critiques also arose concerning issues of exclusion and [[sexual harassment]].<ref name="Alter 2023">{{Cite magazine |last=Alter |first=Charlotte |date=2023-02-03 |title=Effective Altruism Has a Hostile Culture for Women, Critics Say |url=https://time.com/6252617/effective-altruism-sexual-harassment/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230203233205/https://time.com/6252617/effective-altruism-sexual-harassment/ |archive-date=2023-02-03 |access-date=2023-02-04 |magazine=[[Time (magazine)|Time]] |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last=Piper |first=Kelsey |author-link=Kelsey Piper |date=2023-02-15 |title=Why effective altruism is facing allegations around sexual misconduct |url=https://www.vox.com/future-perfect/2023/2/15/23601143/effective-altruism-sexual-harassment-misconduct |access-date=2023-03-29 |website=Vox}}</ref> A 2023 Bloomberg article featured some members of the effective altruism community who alleged that the philosophy masked a culture of predatory behavior.<ref>{{cite web |last=Huet |first=Ellen |date=2023-03-07 |title=Effective altruism's problems go beyond Sam Bankman-Fried |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/features/2023-03-07/effective-altruism-s-problems-go-beyond-sam-bankman-fried |access-date=2023-03-29 |website=[[Bloomberg News]]}}</ref> In a 2023 ''[[Time (magazine)|Time]]'' magazine article, seven women reported misconduct and controversy in the effective altruism movement. They accused men within the movement, typically in the [[San Francisco Bay Area|Bay Area]], of using their power to groom younger women for [[polyamorous]] sexual relationships.<ref name="Alter 2023" /> The accusers argued that the majority male demographic and the polyamorous subculture combined to create an environment where sexual misconduct was tolerated, excused or rationalized away.<ref name="Alter 2023" /> In response to the accusations, the [[Centre for Effective Altruism]] told ''Time'' that some of the alleged perpetrators had already been banned from the organization and said it would investigate new claims.<ref name="Alter 2023" /> The organization also argued that it is challenging to discern to what extent sexual misconduct issues were specific to the effective altruism community or reflective of broader societal [[misogyny]].<ref name="Alter 2023" />

== Other prominent people ==
* [[Sam Altman]], the CEO of OpenAI, has called effective altruism an "incredibly flawed movement" that shows "very weird emergent behavior".<ref name="McMillan 2023">{{Cite web |last1=McMillan |first1=Robert |last2=Seetharaman |first2=Deepa |date=2023-11-22 |title=How Effective Altruism Split Silicon Valley—and Fueled the Blowup at OpenAI |url=https://www.wsj.com/tech/ai/openai-blowup-effective-altruism-disaster-f46a55e8 |access-date=2023-12-23 |website=The Wall Street Journal |language=en-US}}</ref>{{Explain|date=November 2023|reason=What is the behavior that Altman thinks is very weird and why?}} Effective altruist concerns about AI risk were present among the OpenAI board members who fired Altman in November 2023;<ref name="Roberts 2023" /><ref name="McMillan 2023" /><ref>{{cite news|last1=Rai|first1=Saritha|last2=Huet|first2=Ellen|url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2023-11-22/effective-altruism-s-role-in-the-openai-chaos-explained|title=What's Effective Altruism? What Does It Mean for AI?|date=November 22, 2023|newspaper=Bloomberg }}</ref> he has later been reinstated as CEO and the Board membership has changed.<ref>{{multiref2 | {{Cite web |last=Wiggers |first=Kyle |date=2024-03-08 |title=OpenAI announces new board members, reinstates CEO Sam Altman |url=https://techcrunch.com/2024/03/08/openai-announces-new-board-members-reinstates-sam-altman/ |access-date=2024-04-03 |website=TechCrunch |language=en-US}} | {{Cite news |last=Paul |first=Kari |date=2024-03-09 |title=OpenAI reinstates CEO Sam Altman to board after firing and rehiring |url=https://www.theguardian.com/technology/2024/mar/08/openai-sam-altman-reinstated |access-date=2024-04-03 |work=The Guardian |language=en-GB |issn=0261-3077}} }}</ref>
* Actor [[Joseph Gordon-Levitt]] has publicly stated he would like to bring the ideas of effective altruism to a broader audience.<ref name="Lewis-Kraus 2022b" />
* [[Elon Musk]] spoke at an effective altruism conference in 2015.<ref name="Kulish 2022"/>  He described MacAskill's 2022 book ''What We Owe the Future'' as "a close match for my philosophy", but has not officially joined the movement.<ref name="Kulish 2022"/> An article in ''[[The Chronicle of Philanthropy]]'' argued that the record of Musk's substantive alignment with effective altruism was "choppy",<ref>{{cite news |last1=Evans |first1=Nicholas G. |title=Is Elon Musk on Board With 'Effective Altruism'? |url=https://www.philanthropy.com/article/is-elon-muk-on-board-with-effective-altruism |access-date=31 March 2024 |work=The Chronicle of Philanthropy |date=21 April 2023}}</ref> and [[Bloomberg News]] noted that his 2021 charitable contributions showed "few obvious signs that effective altruism ... impacted Musk's giving."<ref>{{cite news |last1=Alexander |first1=Sophie |title=Musk's $5.7 Billion Mystery Gift Went to His Own Charity |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2022-12-12/elon-musk-s-5-7-billion-mystery-gift-went-to-his-own-charity |access-date=31 March 2024 |work=Bloomberg.com |date=12 December 2022 |language=en}}</ref>

* {{cite journal |last1=Earle |first1=Samantha |last2=Read |first2=Rupert |date=March 2016 |title=Effective altruism: Is it effective? Should it be more affective? |journal=[[The Philosophers' Magazine]] |issue=73 |pages=84–91 |doi=10.5840/tpm20167378 }}
* {{cite journal |last=Gabriel |first=Iason |date=August 2016 |title=Effective altruism and its critics |journal=[[Journal of Applied Philosophy]] |volume=34 |issue=4 |pages=457–473 |doi=10.1111/japp.12176 |url=https://www.academia.edu/13786913 |access-date=2017-03-11 |archive-date=2021-02-15 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210215082747/https://www.academia.edu/13786913/Effective_Altruism_and_Its_Critics_Journal_of_Applied_Philosophy_2017_ |url-status=live }}
* {{cite book |editor1-last=Greaves |editor1-first=Hilary |editor1-link=Hilary Greaves |editor2-last=Pummer |editor2-first=Theron |date=2019 |title=Effective Altruism: Philosophical Issues |series=Engaging philosophy |location=Oxford; New York |publisher=[[Oxford University Press]] |isbn=9780198841364 |oclc=1101772304 |doi=10.1093/oso/9780198841364.001.0001 }}
* {{cite journal |last1=Ioannidis |first1=Iraklis |date=Dec 31, 2020 |title=Shackling the poor, or effective altruism: a critique of the philosophical foundation of effective altruism |journal=[[Conatus (journal)|Conatus]] |volume=5 |issue=2 |pages=25–46 |doi=10.12681/cjp.22296 |doi-access=free }}
* {{cite journal |last=Lechterman |first=Theodore M. |date=January 2020 |title=The effective altruist's political problem |journal=Polity |volume=52 |issue=1 |pages=88–115 |doi=10.1086/706867 |url=https://www.academia.edu/37041651 |s2cid=212887647 }}
* {{cite journal |last=MacAskill |first=William |author-link=William MacAskill |date=June 2019c |title=Aid scepticism and effective altruism |journal=Journal of Practical Ethics |volume=7 |issue=1 |pages=49–60 |url=http://philarchive.org/archive/MACASA-4v1 }}
* {{cite journal |last=McMahan |first=Jeff |author-link=Jeff McMahan (philosopher) |date=March 2016 |title=Philosophical critiques of effective altruism |journal=[[The Philosophers' Magazine]] |issue=73 |pages=92–99 |doi=10.5840/tpm20167379 |url=http://jeffersonmcmahan.com/wp-content/uploads/2012/11/Philosophical-Critiques-of-Effective-Altruism-refs-in-text.pdf |access-date=2020-02-05 |archive-date=2021-02-15 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210215082744/http://jeffersonmcmahan.com/wp-content/uploads/2012/11/Philosophical-Critiques-of-Effective-Altruism-refs-in-text.pdf |url-status=live }}
* {{cite book |last=Piper |first=Kelsey |author-link=Kelsey Piper |date=2020 |chapter=Effective altruism |editor1-last=Pigliucci |editor1-first=Massimo |editor1-link=Massimo Pigliucci |editor2-last=Cleary |editor2-first=Skye |editor3-last=Kaufman |editor3-first=Daniel |title=How to Live a Good Life: A Guide to Choosing Your Personal Philosophy |location=New York |publisher=[[Vintage Books]] |pages=255–270 |isbn=9780525566144 |oclc=1133275390}}
* {{cite web |last1=Singer |first1=Peter |author-link1=Peter Singer |last2=Saunders-Hastings |first2=Emma |last3=Deaton |first3=Angus |author-link3=Angus Deaton |last4=Gabriel |first4=Iason |last5=Janah |first5=Leila |author-link5=Leila Janah |last6=Acemoglu |first6=Daron |author-link6=Daron Acemoglu |last7=Brest |first7=Paul |author-link7=Paul Brest |last8=MacFarquhar |first8=Larissa |author-link8=Larissa MacFarquhar |last9=Tumber |first9=Catherine |last10=Reich |first10=Rob |author-link10=Rob Reich |title=Forum: The logic of effective altruism |url=https://bostonreview.net/forum/peter-singer-logic-effective-altruism |website=[[Boston Review]] |date=July 1, 2015 |access-date=February 5, 2020 |archive-date=February 5, 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200205205431/https://bostonreview.net/forum/peter-singer-logic-effective-altruism |url-status=live }} An article based on the preface and first chapter of Singer's book ''[[The Most Good You Can Do]]'' was published in the ''Boston Review'' on July 1, 2015, with a forum of responses by other writers and a final response by Singer.
* {{cite journal |last=Zuolo |first=Federico |date=July 2019 |title=Beyond moral efficiency: effective altruism and theorizing about effectiveness |journal=[[Utilitas]] |volume=32 |pages=19–32 |doi=10.1017/S0953820819000281 |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/333618104 |hdl=11567/1005385 |s2cid=201390996 |access-date=2020-02-05 }}
{{Refend}}
